title: BASICS Model & Resident Needs
summary: Understanding the biopsychosocial model and hierarchy of resident needs.

sections.heading: What is the BASICS Model?
sections.content: BASICS is a biopsychosocial model that assists staff in planning care for residents that supports remaining abilities and caring relationships. Each letter represents a level in the hierarchy of needs: Biological, Activities of Daily Living, Societal, Inter-personal, Creative, Symbolic.

sections.heading: Biological Needs
sections.content: Basic needs for: Food, Water, Oxygen, Safety, Rest, Human Stimulation. When satisfied, fosters Self-Preservation — the resident is physically cared for and safe. Actions to meet biological needs: deliver tray and help prepare meal; provide drinks throughout each shift and remind or encourage the resident to drink them; escort for walks to ensure adequate exercise.

sections.heading: Activities of Daily Living (ADL) Needs
sections.content: Personal needs that support lifestyle: Eating, Mobility, Dressing, Toileting, Personal Hygiene. When satisfied, fosters Self-Dependence — the environment provides supplies and assistance to foster self-performance. Actions: provide two choices of outfit each day; assist with bathing; give verbal prompts to guide ambulation; assist to comb hair during AM care.

sections.heading: Societal Needs
sections.content: Need for unique personal identity, privacy, and resident's place in society. When satisfied, fosters Self-Identity — environment provides for privacy, affiliation with groups, culture, family customs, education, and resident selection of associations. Actions: celebrate birthday; provide quiet place to visit with family; assist with preparation to attend social group; call by preferred name.

sections.heading: Inter-personal Needs
sections.content: Need for connection with others: Love and Belonging. When satisfied, fosters Self-Esteem — the environment fosters social role expression, supports interpersonal and social abilities, promotes social confidence, and creates an atmosphere of caring and being cared about. Actions: encourage telling of personal history with family and staff; introduce to other residents with similar interests; help write a story or draw a picture about things remembered.

sections.heading: Creative Needs
sections.content: Need for personal expression, problem-solving opportunities, and meaningful activity that brings joy. When satisfied, fosters Self-Expression — the environment supports independent activity, humor, creativity, use of talents and skills, and ability to problem-solve and make decisions in everyday life. Actions: look for and support successful problem-solving behavior; provide opportunities to solve meaningful problems; have person play piano or participate in cooking class.

sections.heading: Symbolic Needs
sections.content: Need for expression of beliefs, hopes, dreams, values, and autonomy (ability to control important aspects of life). Sense of peace in the universe according to the resident's belief in the meaning of life. When satisfied, fosters Self-Actualization — environment encourages hopefulness and self-fulfillment, respects expression of the spiritual dimension, and supports spiritual ministry from outside sources as requested. Actions: help to attend religious services; provide consistency and respect for resident's beliefs; maintain a hopeful attitude; provide experiences that help reach some level of peace.

sections.heading: Behaviors Due to Unmet Needs
sections.content: When needs are not met, residents may exhibit: Anxiety, Fear, Anger, Aggressiveness, Depression, Withdrawal. The CNA must identify which need is unmet and take action to meet it.

title: Individuality & Diversity
summary: Recognizing each resident as a unique individual and providing culturally responsive care.

sections.heading: Individuality
sections.content: The particular character, or aggregate of qualities, that distinguishes one person or thing from others; sole and personal nature. It is frequently easy to lose sight of the fact that the resident is an individual human being with personal needs and expectations. There is often a tendency to consider the resident as a subject or just another of a large number of persons in the facility. Proper care requires the establishment of a relationship based upon mutual respect and understanding.

sections.heading: Consequences When Individuality is NOT Recognized
sections.content: Isolation, Alienation (being excluded), Misunderstanding, Mistrust.

sections.heading: Faces of Diversity
sections.content: Diversity includes: Racial classification, National origin, Religious affiliation, Language, Physical size, Gender, Sexual orientation, Age, Disability (both physical and mental), Political orientation, Socio-economic status, Occupational status, Geographical location.

sections.heading: Cultural Desire
sections.content: A genuine passion to be open and flexible with others; to accept differences and build on similarities; to be willing to learn from others as cultural informants. Cultural desire involves the commitment of the nurse aide to care for all residents regardless of their cultural values, beliefs, customs, or practices. When caring for challenging residents who may be in direct moral conflict, see the resident as a unique human being — we all come from the same race: the human race. Our goal in providing culturally responsive care is to find common ground.

sections.heading: Cultural Dimensions that Impact Care
sections.content: Naming: Naming ties individuals to a particular group, place, and tradition. Ask how the resident wishes to be addressed (in Asian cultures it is customary to write the last name first). Closeness: In Hispanic and Middle Eastern cultures, it is customary to move closer to the person speaking. Touch: In China, a slight bow or nod is given at introductions; in Mexico, touch is frequently used when complimenting. Facial Expressions: In some cultures, expressions convey the opposite of what an individual is feeling. Eye Contact: In America/Russia/Poland, direct eye contact is considered honest and attentive; in Native American and Asian cultures it may be considered rude. Use of Silence: To Asians, silence is a sign of respect; in Arabic and English cultures, silence is used for privacy.

title: Communication & Interpersonal Skills
summary: Effective communication with patients, families, and the healthcare team.

sections.heading: Communication Basics
sections.content: Communication is the process people use to exchange information. The information we exchange can be: Facts, Feelings, Opinions, and Ideas. Effective communication is necessary in order for you and your co-workers to help each other satisfy the many needs of residents.

sections.heading: Verbal Communication
sections.content: The oral or written exchange of information. There are 4 parts: the speaker or sender, the message being sent, the listener or receiver of the message, and feedback. Feedback helps gauge how successful we are conveying our messages — we can reflect (repeat back) what was said in our own words or ask questions to clarify.

sections.heading: Nonverbal Communication
sections.content: How you feel about a person, assignment, or task may be communicated nonverbally through: Facial expressions, Body language, Posture, Touch, Tone of Voice. Be aware that your nonverbal communication sends messages even when you are not speaking.

sections.heading: Rules for Communication
sections.content: Try to avoid words with more than one meaning; use familiar words; ask for an explanation of an unfamiliar term; be brief and concise; do not add unrelated or unneeded information; stay on the subject; avoid wandering in thought; do not get wordy; organize your thoughts and present them logically; present facts and be specific.

sections.heading: Tone of Voice
sections.content: The most comfortable tone is in the middle range. High-pitched tones, very low tones, and loud tones (shouting) are difficult for many older adults to hear and are particularly difficult for those with hearing problems.

sections.heading: Barriers to Communication
sections.content: A barrier is anything that prevents the listener's understanding of the message. Barriers can be physical, cultural, and emotional. Common barriers: poor eye contact; ignoring what is being said or interrupting; speaking in another language; use of slang or unfamiliar words; lack of patience or time; feeling stressed or distracted; discussing personal problems; labeling a resident as 'crazy.'

sections.heading: Therapeutic Communication
sections.content: Use open-ended questions. Use silence and active listening. Show empathy and respect. Avoid giving advice, false reassurance, or personal opinions. Do not argue with or belittle a patient. Make eye contact. Use the patient's preferred name. Be sincere and honest. Be patient. Do not rush the resident.

sections.heading: Effective Communication Techniques for Working with Residents
sections.content: Identify yourself when entering a resident's room. Speak clearly and directly to the person when face-to-face. Use words that are familiar to the listener — do not use slang, medical words, or baby talk. Give full attention while maintaining good eye contact and smiling or occasionally nodding. Listen to the resident while they are talking — do not interrupt. Be patient; do not rush the resident.

sections.heading: Active Listening Components
sections.content: Encouraging: convey interest, use neutral words, use various voice intonations. Clarifying: confirm understanding, ask questions, restate wrong interpretation to force speaker to explain. Restating: restate basic ideas and facts to show you are listening. Reflecting: reflect the speaker's basic feelings. Summarizing: restate major ideas expressed including feelings. Validating: acknowledge the value of their issues and feelings; show appreciation for their efforts and actions. 5 Ways to Be a Better Listener: Stop talking; Avoid distractions; Concentrate on what the person is saying; Look for the 'real' meaning; Provide feedback to the sender.

sections.heading: Communicating with Residents with Hearing Impairments
sections.content: Residents with limited hearing may experience isolation and feel 'cut off' from the world. Use a moderate tone of voice. Eliminate background noises. Speak to the resident at eye level — they may be able to read your lips. Provide good lighting. Speak clearly and slowly keeping sentences short. Never shout. Write out important information. Ensure hearing aids are working and reduce background noise.

sections.heading: Communicating with Cognitively Impaired Residents
sections.content: Residents with cognitive impairments lose their ability to relate to the real world and to manage routine ADLs (eating, toileting, bathing). When residents experience a loss of words, they may respond nonverbally by scratching, biting, or hitting. Do not take the resident's behavior toward you personally. Use short phrases and words; make eye contact; use appropriate body language; touch appropriately; reword or rephrase if the resident does not appear to understand.

sections.heading: Communicating with Residents with Aphasia
sections.content: The term aphasic means the resident is able to hear and, in most cases, understand you, but is unable to speak. The resident may try to answer but the words may not come. Face the resident and make eye contact. Say the resident's name before asking questions. Ask simple yes/no questions. Speak clearly using simple words and sentences. Allow the resident to use nonverbal communication — gestures, writing, pointing, chalkboard, word board, picture board.

sections.heading: Communicating with Residents from Diverse Backgrounds
sections.content: Do not assume the resident speaks fluent English. Try to learn some words from the resident's language. Speak clearly and in short sentences. Avoid the tendency to speak louder when not understood (increased volume does not help comprehension). Avoid idioms (phrases usually only understood by native speakers, such as 'it's raining cats and dogs').

sections.heading: Reporting and Documentation
sections.content: Report objective information (what you observe), not subjective interpretation. Reports must be prompt, thorough, and accurate. Always tell the nurse the resident's name, room number, and the time your observations were made or the care was given. Report only things you observed or did yourself. Immediately report any changes from normal or changes in the resident's condition.

sections.heading: General Documentation Guidelines
sections.content: All entries must be legible with correct spelling. Use non-erasable ink. Entries must be initialed; a space is provided for your full signature. Note date and time of any narrative written. Never use whiteout — cross out error and initial it noting 'entry was an error.' Complete documentation for each shift in a timely manner. Use exact measurements whenever possible. Use only accepted abbreviations for the facility. Document facts only — do not add opinions. Never document work that has not been completed; document only after work is done.

sections.heading: Working with the Healthcare Team
sections.content: CNAs work under the supervision of licensed nurses (RN/LPN). Respect chain of command. Never perform tasks outside your scope of practice. Ask for help when unsure. Communicate changes and concerns promptly. Benefits of teamwork: reduced medical errors, improved quality of care, addresses workload issues, builds cohesion, reduces burnout, and generates more creative solutions.

title: Therapeutic Relationship & Resident Transition
summary: Building caring relationships with residents and supporting their transition to long-term care.

sections.heading: Therapeutic Relationship
sections.content: A relationship between an individual who suffers from a disability or discomfort (resident) and a practitioner (caregiver) who possesses a specific technical skill or knowledge and who occupies a recognized role. Goals: to promote communication of distressing thoughts and feelings; to assist the resident with problem solving to help daily living; to promote self-care and independence.

sections.heading: Steps to Establishing a Therapeutic Relationship
sections.content: Be concerned — people tend to trust those who show concern for them. Be competent — people flock to experts, winners, and star performers. Be correct — a track record of accuracy and reliability is critical. Be consistent — words must match policies, performance, principles, and plans over time. Be clear — explaining things that have become second nature to us can be difficult; strive to be clear.

sections.heading: Resident Losses
sections.content: Individuals who enter a long-term care facility face many losses: loss of independence, loss of health, loss of familiar routines/activities, loss of privacy, loss of home and belongings, and in some cases, loss of a spouse. The majority of residents admitted to a long-term care facility are not discharged — the facility becomes their home.

sections.heading: Assisting the Resident Transition
sections.content: Have patience — adjustment time varies for individuals. Allow the resident to voice their opinions, complaints, and questions. Provide support through positive feedback, praise, and reinforcement — recognize and acknowledge each accomplishment with enthusiasm. Provide encouragement to continue looking forward to something meaningful. LISTEN, LISTEN, LISTEN — give the resident every opportunity to voice opinions, questions, and concerns. Do not argue with their perceptions of their new environment.

sections.heading: Promoting Independence and Choice
sections.content: We can improve the quality of life for residents by promoting choice. Residents should choose the direction their care takes, to the extent that they are able. Care that is directed by the resident is more interactive and affirms the resident's dignity and uniqueness. Actions: ensure appropriate adaptive equipment; encourage participation in bathing, dressing, grooming; allow enough time to participate in ADL tasks; offer choices regarding clothing, meals, beverages; encourage participation in activities of choice; encourage the resident to arrange personal items in their room.

title: Infection Control
summary: Preventing the spread of disease in healthcare settings.

sections.heading: Microorganisms and Pathogens
sections.content: Microorganisms are tiny living organisms that can be seen only through a microscope. They are everywhere: in the air, on objects we touch, and in our bodies. Pathogens (germs) are microorganisms that can cause infection. Types: Bacteria (TB, Pneumonia, Strep throat), Viruses (Influenza, Common Cold, Herpes, HIV/AIDS), Fungi (Athlete's Foot, Ringworm, Yeast Infections). A pathogen that easily spreads from one person to another is contagious.

sections.heading: Chain of Infection
sections.content: For an infection to occur, all 6 links must be present in this order: (1) Infectious Agent — the pathogenic microorganism (virus, bacteria, fungi); (2) Reservoir — any place pathogens can live, grow, and multiply (best conditions: warmth, moisture, darkness, food source, oxygen); (3) Portal of Exit — the opening a pathogen uses to leave the body (wound in skin, nose/mouth, bowel/bladder); (4) Mode of Transmission — how infection is carried (contact, droplet, airborne, common vehicle, vector); (5) Portal of Entry — where pathogen enters (respiratory/digestive tract, break in skin, eyes, mucous membranes, urinary/genital tract); (6) Susceptible Host — person whose defenses are weakened. Breaking any link stops the infection.

sections.heading: Modes of Transmission
sections.content: Contact Transmission: through contact with body fluids (tears, saliva, respiratory secretions, perspiration, vomit, urine, feces). Droplet Transmission: coughing, sneezing, talking, singing, laughing, spitting transmit droplets a short distance. Airborne Transmission: germs stay alive in air over greater distances. Common Vehicle Transmission: germs transferred from infected individual to food, water, or equipment then to another person. Vector Transmission: transfer from human to animal (rat, fly, mosquito, roach) then to another human through a bite or non-intact skin.

sections.heading: Standard Precautions
sections.content: Used at ALL times with ALL residents to protect you from exposure to: open skin areas that are not intact, mucous membranes, and blood and body fluids. You should always provide care with the awareness that each resident could be infected with a bloodborne pathogen — an individual could have a virus without showing any signs of illness. Potentially infectious body fluids: Blood, Urine, Feces, Vomit, Tears, Saliva, Sputum, Vaginal Secretions, Semen.

sections.heading: Hand Hygiene
sections.content: Frequent handwashing is the single most important thing you can do to reduce the spread of infection. Wash hands: before and after giving care to each resident; before you start your shift and when you finish; before and after eating or drinking; after using the toilet; before and after breaks; after you cough, sneeze or blow your nose; after handling a resident's belongings; after handling used dressings, specimens, urine or feces; after handling soiled linens; before getting clean linen from a linen cart; before entering a 'clean' supply room. If soap and water are not available, use a hand sanitizer with at least 60% alcohol.

sections.heading: Proper Handwashing Technique
sections.content: 1. Assemble equipment: soap dispenser, paper towels, warm running water, waste container. 2. Stand so clothes do not touch the sink. 3. Turn on faucet; adjust to warm. 4. Wet hands and wrists under running water — keep fingertips lower than elbows so water does not run up your arms. 5. Apply soap; rub hands vigorously for at least 20 seconds, including between fingers and under fingernails. 6. If hands touch the sink at any time, rewash them. 7. Rinse all surfaces of wrists, hands, and fingers with water flowing from arms to fingertips. 8. Carefully remove paper towel from dispenser; thoroughly dry hands. 9. Drop used paper towel into waste container without touching the container. 10. Use a fresh paper towel to turn off faucet; discard the paper towel without touching the waste container.

sections.heading: Transmission-Based Precautions
sections.content: Used in addition to standard precautions for known/suspected infections. Contact Precautions: gown and gloves — for direct contact (hand or skin-to-skin) or indirect contact (touching surfaces/care items); examples: MRSA, C. diff, gastrointestinal/respiratory/skin/wound infections. Droplet Precautions: surgical mask — for infections transmitted by droplets produced by coughing, sneezing, talking; examples: Meningitis, Influenza, Mumps, Rubella. Airborne Precautions: N95 respirator and negative pressure room — for infections transmitted by airborne droplets; examples: Measles, Chickenpox, Tuberculosis.

sections.heading: PPE Donning and Doffing Order
sections.content: Donning (putting on): Gown → Mask/Respirator → Goggles/Face shield → Gloves. Doffing (removing): Gloves → Gown → Mask/Respirator. Perform hand hygiene after each step and after all PPE is removed. Replace worn, punctured, or contaminated gloves. Never wash or decontaminate disposable gloves for reuse. Gowns and masks are worn if there is a possibility of spilling, splashing, or splattering of body fluids, or when caring for a resident in isolation.

sections.heading: Multi-Drug Resistant Organisms (MDROs)
sections.content: MRSA (Methicillin-Resistant Staphylococcus Aureus) and VRE (Vancomycin Resistant Enterococcus) are infections caused by bacteria resistant to many antibiotics. These infections do not respond to traditional antibiotic therapy. Require Contact Precautions — dispose of gown and gloves in the resident's room before going to care for another resident.

sections.heading: Infection Control Program
sections.content: Infection control includes all methods used to prevent and limit the spread of infection. The program contains measures for: Surveillance (data collection, data analysis, data reporting, and decision making), Prevention and Containment (hand hygiene, universal precautions, isolation and barrier precautions), Employee Health (educating employees to protect their own health and prevent workplace infections), Immunizations, and Outbreak Control (every LTC facility should have a framework for managing outbreaks).

sections.heading: Common Infections in Nursing Homes
sections.content: Most common types: Urinary Tract Infections (bladder), Respiratory Tract Infections (lung), Gastrointestinal Tract Infections (diarrhea), Eye Infections, Skin Infections. Factors that reduce the body's ability to fight infection include: poor personal hygiene, poor nutrition, illnesses/disorders that disrupt normal function, breaks in skin or mucous membranes, and some drugs and medical treatments.

sections.heading: Medical Asepsis / Clean vs. Dirty
sections.content: Medical asepsis (clean technique): practices used to remove or destroy pathogens and to prevent their spread from one person or place to another. Clean means free of pathogenic organisms. Dirty means contaminated or soiled. Cleaning is done first (with soap and water or alcohol) to remove large particles, followed by either disinfection or sterilization. Do not share personal grooming articles (combs, toothbrushes, razors). Cover your nose and mouth when sneezing, coughing, or blowing your nose.

sections.heading: Disposal of Contaminated Equipment
sections.content: Sharps Containers: use for needles, broken glass, safety razors — fill only to 3/4 full, then lock and dispose as biohazard/medical waste. Linen and Trash: keep trash and linen hampers covered; wear PPE when changing soiled linen; never leave a soiled brief in the room — dispose immediately; keep clean and dirty linen from touching each other; do not drop dirty linen on the floor; handle contaminated linen as little as possible. Borrowing Equipment: never take equipment from one resident's room to use for another — even if unused, it is considered 'dirty.'

sections.heading: Bloodborne Pathogens and Exposure Control
sections.content: OSHA mandates employers have a written Exposure Plan. Staff at risk must receive training upon employment, yearly, and for new/changed tasks. Preventive measures include: Hepatitis B Vaccination (3 injections — available free to employees) and Standard/Universal Precautions. Work Practice Controls: all tasks involving blood done to limit splattering/spraying; do not eat, drink, smoke, apply cosmetics, or handle contact lenses in areas of occupational exposure; never recap, bend, or remove needles by hand; wash hands after removing gloves. Following an exposure incident: employer makes available a confidential medical evaluation and follow-up including required laboratory testing and post-exposure prophylaxis.

sections.heading: COVID-19 Infection Control
sections.content: COVID-19 is capable of contact, droplet, and airborne transmission in healthcare settings. Employers must follow appropriate precautions for all three transmission pathways. Hand Hygiene: wash with soap and water for at least 20 seconds; use hand sanitizer with at least 60% alcohol when soap/water unavailable. Respiratory Etiquette: cover mouth and nose with tissue when coughing/sneezing; throw used tissues in trash; cough into elbow if no tissue; wash hands immediately after. Facemasks: employers must provide and ensure employees wear facemasks indoors and in vehicles for work purposes; changed at least once per day or when soiled/damaged. Physical Distancing: employees should be separated by at least 6 feet when indoors. Health Screening: employees must be screened before each work day and each shift for COVID-19 symptoms including a temperature check.

title: Vital Signs
summary: Measuring and recording the key indicators of body function.

sections.heading: Temperature
sections.content: Normal oral: 98.6°F / 37°C. Routes: Oral (most common), Rectal (most accurate, 1° higher), Axillary (armpit, 1° lower), Tympanic (ear — measures using the ear), Temporal (forehead). Wait 10-20 minutes after a resident has had a cold or hot drink before taking oral temperature. Report temperature above 100.4°F (fever) or below 96°F (hypothermia).

sections.heading: Pulse
sections.content: Normal adult pulse: 60-100 beats per minute (bpm). Sites: Radial (wrist — most common), Apical (chest), Carotid (neck — used in CPR), Brachial (used for blood pressure). Count for 1 full minute if irregular; 30 seconds × 2 if regular. Note rate, rhythm, and strength.

sections.heading: Respiration
sections.content: Normal adult: 12-20 breaths/minute. One respiration = one inhalation + one exhalation. Count after taking pulse (patient should not know you are counting — counting respirations is done without the resident's awareness). Note rate, rhythm, and depth. Dyspnea = difficulty breathing. Apnea = absence of breathing.

sections.heading: Blood Pressure
sections.content: Normal: less than 120/80 mmHg. Systolic (top number): pressure during heart contraction. Diastolic (bottom number): pressure during relaxation. Hypertension: 130/80 or higher. Hypotension: below 90/60. Use correct cuff size. Place cuff 1 inch above the antecubital space.

sections.heading: Pulse Oximetry
sections.content: Measures oxygen saturation (SpO2) in the blood. Normal: 95-100%. Below 90% is a medical emergency — report immediately. Common probe placement: fingertip. Remove nail polish if needed. Poor circulation can affect readings.

sections.heading: Pain
sections.content: Often called the 5th vital sign. Use a 0-10 numeric scale or the Wong-Baker FACES scale. Always report and document pain. Do not medicate — that is outside CNA scope of practice; report to nurse. Pain while using splints should be reported to the charge nurse promptly.

sections.heading: Weight
sections.content: To obtain the most accurate resident weight, the nursing assistant should weigh the resident at the same time every day. Weight change and swollen legs or feet are warning signs to report (part of STOP and WATCH).

title: Patient Rights & Legal Issues
summary: Understanding the legal and ethical responsibilities of a CNA.

sections.heading: Residents' Rights (Long-Term Care)
sections.content: All residents have the right to: dignity and respect, privacy and confidentiality, make their own decisions (autonomy), refuse treatment, be free from abuse and neglect, participate in care planning, voice grievances without retaliation, have visitors, not have their mail opened by staff, make use of personal belongings only with their permission, and receive care without discrimination.

sections.heading: HIPAA
sections.content: Health Insurance Portability and Accountability Act. Ensures that privacy of personal medical information shared with doctors, hospitals, and others who provide and pay for healthcare is protected. Personal health information must remain confidential and cannot be shared without the individual's authorization. Applies to medical records on paper, in computers, and information communicated orally. Never discuss patients in public areas (hallways, elevators, cafeteria, nurse's station). Do not ever release information to media or newspapers.

sections.heading: Confidentiality
sections.content: Preserving the privacy of residents in your care. All information is considered confidential when it pertains to medical care and resident records — including information gained verbally or from resident records. Confidential information includes: age, sex, race, religion, marital status, occupation, health information, social security number, insurance information, lab tests/X-rays, blood work, MRI/CT scans, any diagnostic procedures, personal care, toileting, and dressing. Breach of confidentiality: sharing information with someone not on the care team or without a release of information form. Consequences of breach: resident embarrassment and emotional distress; irreversible damage to the care relationship; the resident may file charges and sue.

sections.heading: Responsibilities with Confidential Information
sections.content: Discuss resident information ONLY in a place away from other residents, families, and visitors. Report should not be given at the nurses' station. Never discuss residents in an elevator, hallway, cafeteria, or any other public place. Do not ever release information to media or newspapers. Do not keep copies of resident information. Destroy all end-of-shift report sheets after use. Any item with a resident's name or identifying medical information should NEVER be placed in general trash — they should be shredded. Protect privacy when using technology: fax machines, e-mail and computer networks, electronic records, telephones and voice mail, intercom systems.

sections.heading: Abuse and Neglect
sections.content: Types of abuse: Physical, Emotional/Psychological, Sexual, Financial/Material exploitation. Neglect: failure to provide needed care (leaving a resident alone during showering, not providing needed care). False imprisonment: illegally restraining a resident or restricting their freedom (e.g., isolating a resident in a closet). CNAs are mandated reporters — you MUST report any suspected abuse or neglect to a supervisor immediately. Never ignore signs of abuse. An unexplained bruise may indicate abuse — report to the nurse immediately.

sections.heading: Informed Consent
sections.content: Informed consent is when a resident acknowledges and allows the release of information to other parties. This permission is given by filling out a legal consent form, which becomes part of the resident's permanent record. Patients have the right to receive information about their treatment and to consent or refuse. CNAs do not obtain informed consent — that is the nurse's or physician's responsibility. However, always explain what you are going to do before performing any procedure.

sections.heading: Advance Directives
sections.content: Legal documents stating a patient's wishes about medical care if they become unable to make decisions. Includes: Living Will (specific treatment wishes), Durable Power of Attorney for Healthcare (designates a decision-maker), DNR (Do Not Resuscitate). Know your patient's code status before an emergency.

title: Safety & Emergency Procedures
summary: Keeping patients safe and responding to emergencies.

sections.heading: Fall Prevention
sections.content: Lock bed wheels and wheelchair wheels before transfers. Keep bed in lowest position. Use call light within reach. Ensure adequate lighting. Non-slip footwear. Clear pathways. Use gait belt for at-risk patients. Know each patient's fall risk level. If a resident begins to fall during ambulation: help lower the resident to the floor by spreading your feet and bending your knees. Do NOT hang onto the gait belt to try to prevent the fall, as this can cause injury.

sections.heading: Body Mechanics
sections.content: Keep back straight and bend at the knees. Keep load close to your body. Avoid twisting — pivot your feet instead. Keep feet shoulder-width apart for a wide base of support. Push/pull rather than lift when possible. Use mechanical lifts for heavy transfers.

sections.heading: Fire Safety (RACE & PASS)
sections.content: RACE: Rescue anyone in danger → Alarm (pull fire alarm — your FIRST priority as a nursing assistant) → Confine the fire (close doors) → Extinguish/Evacuate. PASS (fire extinguisher): Pull the pin → Aim at base of fire → Squeeze the handle → Sweep side to side. If a small, contained fire breaks out and no one is in immediate danger — after pulling the alarm, you may extinguish the fire if you can do so safely and without harm.

sections.heading: Choking & Heimlich Maneuver
sections.content: Signs of choking: inability to speak, cough, or breathe; clutching throat. Indicated when a resident has a blocked airway. For conscious adult: stand behind patient, place fist above navel, grasp with other hand, give firm upward thrusts. For unconscious: begin CPR and look for the object each time you open the airway.

sections.heading: Restraints
sections.content: Always try least restrictive alternatives first (bed alarms, sitter, padding). Require a physician order and must be regularly monitored. Check every 30 minutes; release and reposition at least every 2 hours. Document use, reason, and patient response. Never tie to side rails.

sections.heading: Emergency Response
sections.content: When a resident becomes short of breath suddenly: call for help and raise the level of the head of the bed immediately. During a code, the best way for a CNA to assist is to retrieve emergency equipment (code cart, intubation box) and carry out other assigned tasks within scope of practice. When an alarm goes off during resident care: place the resident in a safe position and check what caused the alarm immediately.

title: Personal Care & Hygiene
summary: Assisting patients with bathing, grooming, oral care, and dressing.

sections.heading: Oral Care
sections.content: Provide at least twice daily and after meals. For unconscious/comatose patients: be sure the resident's head is turned to its side; use proper equipment to swab the resident's mouth and mucous membranes; suction should be available; never use excess water. Denture care: clean over a sink lined with a towel using cool or tepid water (not hot); carry dentures in gloved hand to the sink; store in labeled container with water when not in use. When providing denture care, line the sink with a paper towel — NOT a washcloth — to reduce the risk of breaking the dentures.

sections.heading: Bathing
sections.content: Water temperature: 105-110°F (41-43°C). Always test water before patient contact. Provide privacy at all times. Work from clean to dirty areas. Wash from distal to proximal on extremities. Perineal care: front to back for females (from urethra to anus) — this prevents fecal bacteria from entering the urethra and causing UTI; rinse the genital area with a fresh washcloth. Respect patient dignity and preferences. Leaving a resident alone during showering without supervision could be considered negligent.

sections.heading: Skin Care & Pressure Ulcer Prevention
sections.content: Inspect skin during every bath. Reposition at least every 2 hours. Keep skin clean and dry. Use moisture barriers for incontinent patients. Do NOT massage reddened areas — this can cause damage. Use pressure-relieving devices (special mattresses, heel protectors). Report any skin changes immediately. Ways to prevent pressure sores: change soiled sheets quickly, change positions regularly, avoid wrinkles in sheets. Providing extra blankets does NOT prevent pressure sores.

sections.heading: Dressing & Grooming
sections.content: Dress affected (weak) side first; undress affected side last (strong side out, weak side in). Allow patient choices to promote independence and dignity. Check for proper footwear. Assist with shaving, hair care, and nail care as directed. Offer two choices of outfit each day.

sections.heading: Elimination Care
sections.content: Provide privacy. Bedpan, urinal, commode, or toilet — use based on patient ability. Measure output if on I&O. Observe and report color, clarity, odor, and amount. Provide perineal care after each elimination episode. Report signs of UTI: burning, frequency, dark or cloudy urine, strong odor.

sections.heading: Care for Residents with Dementia
sections.content: Keep the resident's room uncluttered. Avoid disagreements with the resident. Reassure suspicious residents. Do NOT vary the resident's routines — consistency is key. If a resident refuses bed bath: leave the resident and come back again after some time.

title: Nutrition & Hydration
summary: Assisting patients with meals and monitoring nutritional intake.

sections.heading: Assisting with Meals
sections.content: Wash hands and assist patient with handwashing. Provide oral care before meals if needed. Position patient upright (High Fowler's) for eating. Check tray for correct diet, patient name, and allergies. Allow patient choices. Sit at eye level. Allow adequate time. Do not rush. A resident who requests something to eat late at night: check the records of the resident for her ordered diet and get her something to eat.

sections.heading: Aspiration Prevention
sections.content: Keep head of bed elevated at least 30-45 degrees during and 30-60 minutes after eating. Thickened liquids for patients with swallowing difficulties (dysphagia). Cut food into small pieces. Encourage slow eating. Report coughing, choking, or wet/gurgly voice after eating.

sections.heading: Special Diets
sections.content: Regular: no restrictions. Soft: easy-to-chew foods. Pureed: blended to smooth consistency. Mechanical soft: chopped/minced foods. Low sodium: less than 2,000mg/day. Diabetic/ADA: consistent carbohydrate intake. NPO: nothing by mouth. Always verify the diet order before serving.

sections.heading: Fluid Intake
sections.content: Normal daily intake: 2,000-3,000 mL. Offer fluids frequently throughout the day. Encourage fluids unless on restriction. Record intake in mL if patient is on I&O monitoring. 8 oz = approximately 240 mL. Signs of dehydration: dry mouth, dark urine, dizziness, confusion. Drinking less is a warning sign to report (part of STOP and WATCH).

title: Promoting Resident's Independence
summary: Strategies to support resident choice, independence, and quality of life in long-term care.

sections.heading: Why Independence Matters
sections.content: The majority of residents admitted to a long-term care facility are not discharged — the facility becomes their home. Being independent promotes healthy self-esteem. There is a common expression: 'If you don't use it, you lose it.' This is particularly true of self-help skills. We are not doing residents any favors by performing skills that they are able to complete themselves.

sections.heading: Promoting Choice
sections.content: We can improve quality of life by promoting choice for residents. Making decisions increases the sense of independence and aids in enhancing self-esteem. We should provide our residents with opportunities to make choices throughout the day. Care that is directed by the resident is more interactive and affirms each individual's dignity and uniqueness. Resident-centered care means that care is delivered to meet the individual needs of each resident's schedule, rather than the schedule that the staff determines.

sections.heading: How to Promote Resident Choice
sections.content: Get to know the resident — speak with your residents about what their life has been like, their likes and dislikes, and what routines they have followed. You will be able to help them keep these routines. Encourage the resident to tell you how best to care for them — often the resident knows the best and easiest way to be cared for. For example: he may know the best way to be bathed so he is not in pain; she may know that it is easier if her left arm is put into the sleeve first. This will make the task easier and take less time to care for the resident. Getting to know residents well will help you. Some residents, angry at being unable to live at home, may take it out on you. The more you speak to the residents, the more they will see you as a person — they will be less likely to take their dissatisfactions out on you.

sections.heading: Encouraging Daily Routine and Self-Choice
sections.content: Encourage residents to choose their daily routine. If a resident does not want to get up early and needs help to dress, give her time by taking care of other residents first who do not mind getting up early. If a resident wants to take a bath in the evening, help work out such a schedule by trying to switch other jobs from evening to daytime — work with other caregivers to make this happen. Encourage residents to choose things for themselves — go to the closet and ask the resident to choose what they want to wear. Encourage the resident to decide what types of activities they want to be involved in and help them get to them. Some residents complain or get angry because they are frustrated — they are frustrated because they cannot do what they want to do. By making this possible for them, you may find that they will act differently.

sections.heading: What Might Stand in the Way of Resident Independence
sections.content: Encouraging residents to be as independent as possible may be limited by: a desire not to make things too difficult for yourself (it may be easier to brush a resident's teeth than to take the time to help the resident do it themselves, or it may be easier to give someone a bath than to arrange things so the resident can wash with assistance); family members who may not want their relative to be independent because they are so concerned for their relative's safety; some residents may be afraid of doing things they believe they could do safely; residents who do not speak English may find it hard to communicate and understand. Giving residents time to do things themselves may take time in the beginning, but in the end, they will be doing things that you would have had to do.

sections.heading: Ways to Encourage Resident Independence
sections.content: Encourage the resident to do it themselves. Make sure the resident has what they need to be as independent as possible — keep walkers, canes and wheelchairs ready to be used (if these are not available, the resident will have to call for help). Make sure that any devices the resident needs to eat are at their side — if these are not available, the resident will be unable to feed themselves and will need you by their side. Support the resident who is afraid — if you know that a resident can safely walk by themselves for a short distance or safely wash themselves once in the shower, but is afraid, reassure them that you will be with them if they need help; encourage them to try for themselves. Encourage families to support the resident's independence — help families understand that if residents do not do things for themselves wherever they can, they will quickly become more dependent; make the family feel comfortable by asking a supervisor to explain that it is safe for their relative to be independent.

sections.heading: Quality of Life and Quality of Care
sections.content: Quality of Care is a measure of the degree to which delivered health care services meet established professional standards and judgments of value by the consumer. Quality of Life is related to a person's sense of well-being, level of satisfaction with life, and feeling of self-worth and self-esteem. For nursing home residents this includes a basic sense of satisfaction with oneself, the environment, the care received, the accomplishment of desired goals, and control over one's life. Many aspects of nursing home life that affect a resident's perceptions of quality of life — and therefore sense of well-being — are intimately intertwined with quality of care.

sections.heading: Whole Person Approach to Care
sections.content: The focus is on the well-being of the whole resident, not just the disease process. Care is designed to meet needs in all realms: Physical, Psychological, Social, and Spiritual. Care techniques to fulfill basic human needs: listen with interest to what the resident is saying; assist with bathing and personal hygiene preferences without interfering with independence; assist with dressing in appropriate clothing and the resident's choice; promote resident independence and dignity with dining; close door and use privacy screens when providing care.

title: Abuse, Neglect & Exploitation
summary: Recognizing, responding to, and reporting abuse, neglect, and exploitation of nursing home residents.

sections.heading: Residents' Rights Regarding Abuse
sections.content: The resident has the right to be free from abuse, neglect, misappropriation of resident property, and exploitation. This includes, but is not limited to, freedom from corporal punishment, involuntary seclusion and any physical or chemical restraint not required to treat the resident's symptoms. Residents must not be subjected to abuse by anyone including: facility staff, other residents, consultants, volunteers, staff of other agencies, family members or legal guardians, friends, or any other individuals.

sections.heading: Abuse Defined
sections.content: Abuse is the willful infliction of injury, unreasonable confinement, intimidation, or punishment with resulting physical harm, pain or mental anguish. Abuse also includes the deprivation by an individual, including a caretaker, of goods or services that are necessary to attain or maintain physical, mental, and psychosocial wellbeing. It includes verbal abuse, sexual abuse, physical abuse, and mental abuse including abuse facilitated or enabled through the use of technology. Staff to Resident Abuse: it is the facility's responsibility to ensure that all staff are trained and knowledgeable in how to react and respond appropriately to resident behavior. All staff are expected to be in control of their own behavior and to behave professionally. It is not acceptable for an employee to claim their action was 'reflexive' or a 'knee-jerk reaction.' Retaliation by staff is abuse, regardless of whether harm was intended.

sections.heading: Physical Abuse
sections.content: Physical abuse includes, but is not limited to, hitting, slapping, punching, biting, and kicking. Corporal punishment — physical punishment used as a means to correct or control behavior — is also physical abuse. Examples: Hitting, Pinching, Slapping, Punching, Shoving, Grabbing, Kicking, Biting, Pulling Hair, Shaking, Rough Handling, Restraints. Possible indicators of physical abuse: an injury that is suspicious because the source is not observed, the extent or location of the injury is unusual, or because of the number of injuries; bruises in unusual locations such as the head, neck, lateral arms, posterior torso or trunk, or bruises in shapes such as finger imprints; facial injuries including broken or missing teeth, facial fractures, black eye(s), bruising, bleeding or swelling of the mouth or cheeks; fractures, sprains, or dislocations; burns, blisters, or scalds on the hands or torso; bite marks, scratches, skin tears and lacerations.

sections.heading: Mental and Verbal Abuse
sections.content: Mental abuse is the use of verbal or nonverbal conduct which causes or has the potential to cause the resident to experience humiliation, intimidation, fear, shame, agitation, or degradation. Verbal abuse includes the use of oral, written, or gestured communication, or sounds, to residents within hearing distance, regardless of age, ability to comprehend, or disability. Examples: harassing a resident; mocking, insulting, ridiculing; yelling or hovering over a resident with intent to intimidate; threatening residents including threatening to withhold care or withholding a resident from contact with family and friends; isolating a resident from social interaction or activities; keeping and/or distributing demeaning or humiliating photographs and recordings through social media or multimedia messaging. If a photograph or recording of a resident demeans or humiliates the resident, it would be considered abuse regardless of whether the resident provided consent and regardless of the resident's cognitive status.

sections.heading: Sexual Abuse
sections.content: Sexual abuse is non-consensual sexual contact of any type with a resident. Sexual contact is nonconsensual if the resident either appears to want the contact but lacks the cognitive ability to consent, or does not want the contact to occur. Examples include: unwanted intimate touching of any kind especially of breasts or perineal area; all types of sexual assault or battery such as rape, sodomy, and coerced nudity; forced observation of masturbation and/or pornography; taking sexually explicit photographs and/or audio/video recordings of a resident and maintaining and/or distributing them. Indicators of potential sexual abuse: bruises around the breasts, genital area, or inner thighs; unexplained sexually transmitted disease or genital infections; unexplained vaginal or anal bleeding; torn, stained, or bloody underclothing. Psychosocial outcomes of sexual abuse: depression, anxiety, and post-traumatic stress disorder. Sudden or unexplained changes such as fear or avoidance of a person or place, or feelings of guilt or shame may also indicate abuse.

sections.heading: Neglect
sections.content: Neglect means the failure of the facility, its employees, or service providers to provide goods and services to a resident that are necessary to avoid physical harm, pain, mental anguish or emotional distress. Staff has the knowledge and ability to provide care and services, but choose not to do it, or acknowledge the request for assistance from a resident(s), which result in care deficits. Neglect of goods or services may occur when staff are aware, or should be aware, of residents' care needs based on assessment and care planning, but are unable to meet the identified needs. Examples of neglect: purposely withholding assistance, food, fluids; ignoring calls or call lights; making the resident 'wait' for your convenience; not feeding a resident who needs assistance with meals. Circumstances that may lead to neglect: lack of training to perform an intervention; lack of sufficient staffing; lack of supplies; or staff lack of knowledge of the needs of the resident.

sections.heading: Misappropriation of Property and Exploitation
sections.content: Misappropriation of resident property means the deliberate misplacement, exploitation, or wrongful, temporary, or permanent use of a resident's belongings or money without the resident's consent. Exploitation means taking advantage of a resident for personal gain, through the use of manipulation, intimidation, threats, or coercion. Examples of resident property: jewelry, clothing, furniture, money, electronic devices, credit cards, bank accounts, driver's licenses, and social security cards. Examples of misappropriation: identity theft; theft of money from bank accounts; unauthorized or coerced purchases on a resident's credit card; a resident who provides a gift to staff in order to receive ongoing care based on staff's persuasion; the diversion of a resident's medication(s), including controlled substances for staff use or personal gain. Residents are permitted to keep personal clothing and possessions for their use while in the facility, as long as it does not infringe upon the rights of other residents.

sections.heading: Involuntary Seclusion
sections.content: Involuntary seclusion is defined as separation of a resident from other residents or from their room (with or without roommates) against the resident's will or the will of the resident representative. Involuntary seclusion may take many forms, including but not limited to the confinement, restriction or isolation of a resident. It may be a result of staff convenience, a display of power from the caregiver, or used to discipline a resident for wandering, yelling, repeatedly requesting care or services, using the call light, or disrupting a program or activity. Examples: a resident displays disruptive behaviors and staff seclude the resident in an office area or their room; staff place furniture, carts, or chairs in front of doorways to prevent egress; a resident is physically placed in an area without access to call lights, creating an environment of seclusion and isolation for the resident.

sections.heading: Injuries of Unknown Source and Identification
sections.content: An injury should be classified as an 'injury of unknown source' when both of the following conditions are met: the source of the injury was not observed by any person or the source of the injury could not be explained by the resident; and the injury is suspicious because of the extent or location of the injury, or because of the number of injuries observed at one particular point in time or the incidence of injuries over time. Recognition of unreported abuse: an injury that is suspicious because the source of the injury is not observed or the extent/location is unusual; sudden or unexplained changes in behaviors and/or activities such as fear of a person or place, or feelings of guilt or shame.

sections.heading: What to Do If Abuse Is Occurring
sections.content: If an incident of abuse is discovered to be actively occurring, the resident should be immediately protected and removed from the situation. Three actions: (1) STOP the abuse; (2) PROTECT the resident from additional abuse; (3) REPORT to a Supervisor. The person observing an incident of resident abuse or suspected resident abuse must immediately report such incident to the supervisor. The supervisor must notify the Director of Nursing and Administrator immediately. The following information should be reported: the name of the resident(s) involved; the date and time the incident occurred; where the incident took place; the name(s) of the person(s) committing the incident if known; the name(s) of any witnesses; the type of abuse committed (verbal, physical, sexual, neglect, etc.); other information that may be requested by the charge nurse.

sections.heading: Investigation and Protection
sections.content: The facility must thoroughly investigate an alleged violation of abuse, neglect, exploitation, or mistreatment. The investigation must begin immediately regardless of the time of day. The Administrator and/or Director of Nursing will complete the investigation. The facility must prevent further abuse while the investigation is in progress and take appropriate corrective action. As the facility conducts its investigation, it must not tamper with evidence — tampering would impede completion of a thorough investigation. The facility must ensure all residents are protected from physical and psychosocial harm during and after the investigation, including: responding immediately to protect the alleged victim; examining the alleged victim for any sign of injury; increased supervision of the alleged victim and residents; room or staffing changes if necessary; providing emotional support and counseling to the resident.

sections.heading: Reporting Requirements and Consequences
sections.content: All alleged violations involving abuse, neglect, exploitation, or mistreatment are reported immediately, but not later than 2 hours after the allegation is made if events involve serious bodily injury, or not later than 24 hours if the events do not involve abuse and do not result in serious bodily injury, to the Administrator and NYS DOH. Report results of all investigations to the Administrator and the State Survey Agency within 5 working days of the incident. In addition, the facility must notify local law enforcement of any reasonable suspicion of a crime. Consequences for the abuser: immediate dismissal, civil penalties, name entered into the nurse aide registry. Consequences for failure to report abuse: suspension and/or dismissal, civil penalties, legal ramifications. Under New York State Public Health Law Section 2803-d, a nurse aide found guilty of neglect, abuse, misappropriation of property, or exploitation by a court of law must have their name entered into the State Nurse Aide Registry.

sections.heading: Abuse-Free Resident Care
sections.content: Providing abuse-free care involves every aspect of the care you provide to residents. This includes: keeping the environment safe and furniture in residents' rooms placed to aid their independence; providing adequate food and fluids, given safely and with assistance appropriate for feeding an adult; providing personal care (bathing, dressing, grooming, etc.) gently, thoroughly, and appropriately for an adult; helping the resident to regain function and be as independent as health condition permits; meeting communication, socialization and emotional needs in a kind, gentle manner; treating residents with dignity, respect and kindness, and honoring residents' rights and preferences; meeting and being sensitive to social service and mental health needs of residents, even those with memory and behavior problems or individuals with different cultural backgrounds. Staff Who May React Abusively Toward Residents: are sick and tired; have worked too many hours; are under stress in their personal lives; are drug or alcohol abusers; have poor supervision; have low self-esteem; are repeatedly working 'short-staffed'; do not get along well with co-workers; take things personally; tend to be impatient or rough, or to rush residents who are cognitively impaired; tend not to use a calm, friendly, non-verbal approach; may appear 'threatening' to residents.

title: Circulatory & Respiratory Systems
summary: Understanding the heart, blood vessels, and lungs — common diseases, aging changes, and the CNA's role.

sections.heading: The Cardiovascular (Circulatory) System
sections.content: The circulatory system (aka the cardiovascular system) consists of: the heart, the blood, and blood vessels (arteries and veins; capillaries and lymph vessels). The main function is circulating blood through the body. The heart pumps the blood and moves oxygen, food, and other life-sustaining nutrients through the body. Common physical changes during aging — Cardiovascular: heart pumps with less force; heart valves thicken and become stiff; heart rate may slow; abnormal heart rhythms may occur; heart may enlarge slightly; heart walls thicken; arteries narrow and become stiffer; less blood flows through narrowed arteries; weakened heart works harder to pump blood through narrowed vessels; number of red blood cells decreases.

sections.heading: Myocardial Infarction (Heart Attack)
sections.content: Myocardial Infarction (MI) is the medical term for a heart attack. A heart attack is caused by an inadequate supply of blood to the heart, usually due to a blood clot. An MI is actual tissue death within the heart muscle. Signs and Symptoms of MI: severe chest pain in the center of the chest that may radiate to the left jaw and left arm — pain may also be felt in the center of the back or in the shoulders; shortness of breath; sweating; blue color to lips from decreased oxygen; vomiting or complaints of indigestion; low blood pressure. Treatment includes medicine and oxygen — the resident will require immediate emergency attention and transfer to a hospital.

sections.heading: Hypertension (High Blood Pressure)
sections.content: Hypertension is high blood pressure. Causes include lifestyle behaviors, heredity, and other contributing factors. Narrowing and hardening of the blood vessels due to a build-up of fatty plaques on the inside vessel walls is the most common cause. Signs and Symptoms of HTN: blurred vision, headache, nosebleed, chest pain, flushed face, weak and thready pulse, clammy skin. Treatment may include a low-fat, low-sodium diet and medications. Lifestyle changes such as increasing exercise, quitting smoking (nicotine narrows the blood vessels), and stress management are other measures that help control blood pressure.

sections.heading: Congestive Heart Failure (CHF)
sections.content: Congestive Heart Failure (CHF) results when the heart cannot effectively pump blood. This causes the fluids of the system to back up into the lungs and extremities. Signs and Symptoms of CHF: shortness of breath; restlessness and anxiety; edema (swelling) of the legs, hands, feet, and face; chest pain; congestion in the lungs, non-productive cough; weight gain from fluid retention.

sections.heading: Angina
sections.content: Angina is pain or discomfort due to lack of oxygen to the heart muscle. It may manifest as pain or discomfort, heaviness, tightness, pressure or burning, numbness, aching, tingling in the chest, back, neck, throat, jaw, or arms. Treatment options include rest, medications, angioplasty, or coronary artery bypass graft surgery (CABG).

sections.heading: Peripheral Vascular Disease (PVD)
sections.content: Peripheral Vascular Disease (PVD) is a narrowing of vessels that carry blood to the legs, arms, stomach, or kidneys. Treatment includes medications, possible surgery to dilate blood vessels, or lifestyle changes: stop smoking, control diabetes, control blood pressure, exercise, a low-fat, low-cholesterol diet.

sections.heading: The Respiratory System
sections.content: The respiratory system consists of: the nose, oral cavity, pharynx (throat), larynx (voice box), trachea (windpipe), right and left bronchi, bronchioles, alveoli, right and left lung, and diaphragm. The respiratory system brings oxygen into the lungs through inhalation (breathing in) and removes carbon dioxide from the body through exhalation (breathing out). Oxygen is carried through the body by the bloodstream. Common physical changes during aging — Respiratory: respiratory muscles weaken; some lung tissue is lost; lung tissue becomes less elastic; chest is less able to stretch and breathe; difficulty breathing (dyspnea); decreased strength for coughing and clearing the airway.

sections.heading: Pneumonia
sections.content: Pneumonia is an acute disease that can be caused by bacteria, viruses, aspiration, and immobility. Pneumonia causes the airways to fill with fluid and obstructs the respiratory system with inflammation of the lung tissue, impairing breathing. Signs and Symptoms: difficulty breathing; shortness of breath; cough, sometimes with increased sputum; rapid respiration and pulse rate; change in mental status caused by decreasing levels of oxygen in the blood (hypoxic); dehydration; chest pain or tightness especially when inhaling. Treatment can include antibiotics, pain relievers, steroids, and medications to decrease fever; oxygen therapy may be used; hospitalization may be required.

sections.heading: Asthma
sections.content: Asthma is an obstructive disorder that causes blockage of the bronchi through inflammation. The overall effect is limiting the breathing process. Asthma can result from smoking, allergic reactions, or emotional stress. Signs and Symptoms: rapid respirations; difficulty breathing; wheezing; rapid heart rate; perspiration; coughing. Treatment can include bronchodilators (medications to open the bronchi); in some cases steroids or antibiotics.

sections.heading: Emphysema
sections.content: Emphysema is a disease of the alveoli, the smallest part of the bronchial tree. The alveoli become permanently enlarged, trapping air in the sacs and preventing air exchange from taking place. Signs and Symptoms: anxiety caused by limited breathing capacity; weight loss; barrel chest due to loss of elasticity of the lung; leaning forward to breathe; chronic cough; wheezing. Treatment includes medications to dilate the airway, nebulizer treatments to increase breathing capacity, and oxygen.

sections.heading: Influenza (Flu)
sections.content: Influenza, commonly known as the flu, is an infectious disease caused by a virus. Common symptoms are fever, sore throat, muscle pains, severe headache, coughing, weakness, and general discomfort. In more serious cases, influenza causes pneumonia. Treatment includes plenty of rest, drinking a lot of liquids, and if necessary, taking medications such as acetaminophen to relieve fever and muscle aches.

sections.heading: Tuberculosis (TB)
sections.content: Tuberculosis (TB) is an infectious disease caused by a bacteria. When the inhaled tuberculosis bacteria enter the lungs, they can multiply and cause a local lung infection (pneumonia). The local lymph nodes associated with the lungs may also become involved and usually become enlarged. TB is spread usually from person to person by breathing infected air during close contact. Active TB is treated with a combination of medications. Treatment usually lasts for many months and sometimes for years.

sections.heading: Oxygen Therapy
sections.content: Oxygen is treated as a medication. The doctor must order the amount of oxygen to give and the device to use. The order also states if oxygen is given continuously or intermittently. Oxygen Sources: wall outlets (oxygen piped into each resident's room connected to a centrally located supply); oxygen tanks (portable — the gauge tells how much oxygen is left); oxygen concentrators (remove oxygen from the air — a power source is needed); liquid oxygen systems (portable unit filled from a stationary unit). Devices used to administer oxygen: Nasal Cannula — two prongs project from tubing and are inserted a short distance into the nostrils; the resident can eat and talk; nasal irritation and pressure on the ears is possible. Simple Face Mask — covers the nose and mouth; the mask has small holes in the side; carbon dioxide escapes during exhalation; room air enters during inhalation. Non-Rebreathing Face Mask — prevents air from entering the reservoir bag; exhaled air leaves through holes in the mask; oxygen from the reservoir bag is inhaled; the bag must not totally collapse during exhalation. Observations to Report When on Oxygen Therapy: frequent headaches; anxiety; cyanotic (blue) lips or fingernails; drowsiness; confusion; restlessness; slow, shallow, difficult, or irregular breathing (hypoxic — lack of oxygen).

sections.heading: Measuring and Recording Vital Signs
sections.content: Temperature, pulse, and respirations (abbreviated TPR) are important indicators of a person's internal condition. Normal vital signs: Temperature — Oral 98.6°F (normal range 97.6°F–99.6°F), Rectal 99.6°F (normal range 98.6°F–100.6°F), Axillary (armpit) 97.6°F (normal range 96.6°F–98.6°F), Tympanic (ear) 98.6°F, Temporal Artery (temple) 99.6°F. Pulse: 60–100 beats/min. Respirations: 12–20/min. Blood Pressure: Systolic 100–120, Diastolic 70–80. Factors affecting vital signs: activity, age, anger, anxiety, drugs, eating, exercise, fear, gender, illness, noise, pain, sleep, smoking, stress, weather, weight. Unless otherwise ordered, take vital signs with the person at rest — lying or sitting. Vital signs show even minor changes in the person's condition. Accuracy is essential when you measure, record, and report vital signs.

sections.heading: Radial Pulse and Respirations
sections.content: The most common site for checking the pulse is on the inside of the wrist where the radial artery runs just below the skin — this is called the radial pulse. Place the first 2 or 3 fingertips of one hand against the radial artery (on the thumb side of the wrist). Press fingers firmly enough to feel the pulse easily. Note if the pulse is strong or weak, regular or irregular. Count beats for 60 seconds. Pulse Rhythm: the pulse rhythm should be regular — the same interval occurs between beats; an irregular pulse occurs when the beats are not evenly spaced or beats are skipped. Pulse Force: a forceful pulse is described as strong, full, or bounding; hard-to-feel pulses are described as weak, thready, or feeble. Respirations: count respirations right after taking a pulse — keep your fingers over the pulse site; to count respirations, watch the chest rise and fall; count for 1 minute. Do not tell the resident you are counting respirations, as people tend to change their breathing patterns when they know they are being counted.

sections.heading: Taking and Recording Temperatures
sections.content: Body temperature is the amount of heat in the body. It is a balance between the amount of heat produced and the amount of heat lost by the body. Body temperature is lower in the morning and higher in the afternoon and evening. Older persons have lower body temperatures — an oral temperature of 98.6°F may signal fever in an older person. Fever means an elevated body temperature (Febrile = with a fever; Afebrile = without a fever). Temperatures are NOT taken orally if the resident: is unconscious; has had surgery or an injury to the face, neck, nose or mouth; is using oxygen via facemask; is confused or disoriented; has a history of seizure disorders. Temperature Sites: mouth, rectum, axilla (underarm), tympanic membrane (ear), temporal artery (forehead). Types of thermometers: Glass (mercury-free), Electronic Thermometer (standard, tympanic, temporal artery), Disposable. Oral and rectal thermometers can be identified by shape and color markings — oral thermometers have slender bulbs and may be blue or green; rectal thermometers have round or stubby bulbs and may be red. Tympanic thermometers take fast, accurate temperature readings — the tip only goes in ¼ to ½ inch into the ear canal. Always report temperatures above or below the normal range for a resident.

sections.heading: Measuring Blood Pressure
sections.content: Blood pressure is the force of the blood that pushes against the walls of the blood vessels. When taking the resident's blood pressure, you are measuring the force of the blood flowing through the arteries. Equipment: stethoscope and blood pressure cuff (sphygmomanometer). The stethoscope is an instrument used to listen to sounds produced by the body — it makes body sounds easier to hear. Parts of the blood pressure cuff: Dial (shows numbers from 20 to 300, each line represents 2 millimeters of mercury), Cuff (contains a cloth-covered rubber bag), Valve (thumbscrew opens and closes the valve controlling air in and out of cuff), Bulb (allows air to enter the rubber bag when squeezed). Do not take blood pressure in the resident's arm that: is injured or paralyzed; has a cast; has an intravenous infusion or site; is on the same side as a mastectomy. Reminders: have the resident sit and rest for 15 minutes before taking blood pressure; ask resident not to talk; remove clothing from the arm; wrap the cuff snugly around the arm; if unsure of the reading, deflate the cuff and wait 5 minutes before taking it again. Normal Adult Blood Pressure Range: Systolic 100–120, Diastolic 70–80.

sections.heading: Measuring Height and Weight
sections.content: A resident's height is measured on admission to the facility and yearly. Height can be obtained using a standing scale or when lying in bed using a tape measure. Weight is vital in the care of the resident. Weight is measured weekly for 4 weeks when a resident is admitted or re-admitted to the facility and then at least monthly. Be sure to report any significant loss or gain — a general guideline is a weight gain or loss of 2 to 3 pounds. It is important every time to measure a resident's weight at the same time of day, using the same scale, with the resident wearing the same type and amount of clothing. Types of scales: standing scale and chair scale (for residents who cannot stand). For standing scale: start with the scale balanced at zero; make sure resident is wearing nonskid shoes fastened before walking; help resident to stand on the center of the scale, be sure they are not holding, touching, or leaning against anything; determine the resident's weight by balancing the scale bar level. For chair/wheelchair scale: start with the scale balanced at zero; help the resident transfer into the chair scale, or roll the wheelchair onto the scale; determine the resident's weight — subtract the weight of the wheelchair from the total and record the answer as the resident's weight.

title: The Resident's Environment
summary: Room equipment, elements of comfort, resident rights in their environment, and infection control for linens.

sections.heading: Room Overview
sections.content: Resident rooms must be comfortable, safe, and allow enough space for activities of daily living. Rooms are designed to provide comfort, safety, and privacy. The facility is now the person's home — you must help the resident feel safe, secure, and comfortable. A home-like setting is important to promote quality of life.

sections.heading: Room Equipment
sections.content: The Bed: Has electric and manual controls. Raised horizontally to give care. Head raised to varying degrees. Bed legs have wheels/casters that must be locked when giving care, transferring, or when the resident is lying in bed. The Overbed Table: Positioned by sliding the base under the bed. Height adjustable. Used for meal trays, writing, and other activities. Must be cleaned before and after use. Never place bedpans, urinals, or soiled linen on it. The Bedside Stand: Storage area for personal items and personal care equipment. Usually has a drawer and two shelves. Top drawer for eyeglasses, books, and personal items. First shelf for wash basin, soap, lotion, deodorant. Lower shelf holds the bedpan, urinal cover, and toilet paper.

sections.heading: Other Room Components
sections.content: Chairs: The resident's room usually has one or two chairs. Privacy Curtains: Pulled around the bed while care is given. They do NOT block sound or prevent conversations from being heard. The Call System: Allows the resident to signal when help is needed. Must always be attached to the bed or chair and within the resident's reach. The Bathroom: Toilet, sink, call system, and mirror are standard equipment. Hand rails are installed for safety. Closet and Drawer Space: Must have shelves and a clothes rack. The resident must have free access to the closet and its contents.

sections.heading: Elements of Comfort
sections.content: Four key elements affect resident comfort: Temperature and Ventilation (room should be comfortably warm, free of drafts), Odors (rooms should be odor-free; report any unusual odors to the supervisor), Noise (reduce unnecessary noise; it causes stress and sleep disruption), and Lighting (adequate lighting for safety and activities; avoid glare).

sections.heading: Resident Rights & OBRA Requirements
sections.content: OBRA requires that resident rooms be as homelike as possible. Residents are allowed to have and use personal items including furniture, family photos, religious items, and books. Some may have plants to care for. Residents are allowed personal choice in arranging personal items as long as choices are safe. According to OBRA, residents have the right to full visual privacy — a resident must have means to be completely free from public view while occupying his or her bed. The privacy curtain provides this. Residents also have the right to locked storage space.

sections.heading: Infection Control for Linens
sections.content: Key linen safety rules: Dispose of dirty gloves in the room. Never leave a soiled brief in the room — dispose of them immediately. If a clean piece of linen drops on the floor, it becomes dirty and must be put in the isolation linen hamper labeled with the BIOHAZARD symbol. Keep clean and dirty linen from touching each other. Do not drop dirty linen on the floor. Handle contaminated linen as little as possible and avoid shaking (which transfers germs to the air). Contaminated items are bagged to remove them from a resident's room — use leak-proof plastic bags.

title: Admission, Transfer & Discharge
summary: Supporting residents through the challenging transitions of admission, room transfers, and discharge.

sections.heading: Adjusting to Long-Term Care
sections.content: Moving always requires adjustment, and as a person ages it can be even harder. This is especially true if illness, disability, and mobility problems are present. CNAs play an important role in helping residents make a successful transition. By giving emotional support such as listening and being kind, compassionate, and helpful, CNAs can make residents feel better about their new homes.

sections.heading: Admission
sections.content: Admission is the first time a CNA meets a new resident. This is the time of first impressions — the CNA should help the resident have a good impression of him and the facility. Staff must communicate with new residents. CNAs can explain what to expect during the process and answer any questions within their scope of practice. It is a good idea to ask new residents about their personal preferences and routines. If residents are not able to respond, ask the family.

sections.heading: Admission Guidelines
sections.content: Prepare the room before the resident arrives: make sure the bed is made and the room is tidy, restock supplies, and make sure an admission kit is available. When the new resident arrives, note the time and condition — are they using a wheelchair, stretcher, or walking? Who is with them? Observe for level of consciousness and confusion. Look for signs of nervousness. Note any tubes such as IVs or catheters. Introduce yourself, state your position, smile, and be friendly. Always call the person by their formal name until they tell you what they want to be called. Never rush the process — make sure the new resident feels welcome and wanted.

sections.heading: Settling the New Resident In
sections.content: Explain day-to-day life in the facility. Offer to take the resident and family on a tour. Show them important areas including where the dining room is and review dining schedules. During the tour, introduce the resident to other residents and staff members. Introduce the roommate if there is one. Handle personal items with care and respect — residents have a legal right to have personal items treated carefully. Place personal items where the resident wants them. Let residents adapt to their new homes at their own pace. Report signs of confusion or depression to the nurse.

sections.heading: Transferring a Resident
