sections.content: Residents may be transferred to a different area of the facility, or in cases of acute illness, to a hospital. Change is difficult — especially when a person has an illness or condition that worsens. Staff should make the transfer as smooth as possible. A resident should be informed of the transfer as soon as possible. The nurse or social worker will tell the resident about the transfer and explain how, where, when, and why. All questions should be answered. CNAs help residents pack personal items before transferring. Involve residents in the packing process — let them see the empty closet, drawer, etc.

sections.heading: Discharging a Resident
sections.content: To discharge a resident from the facility, a doctor must give the discharge order. The nurse then completes instructions for the resident to follow after discharge and will review these instructions with the resident and family. The day of discharge is often a happy day for residents going home. However, some residents may feel uncertainty or fear about leaving the facility and may be concerned that their health will suffer. CNAs help by collecting the resident's belongings and packing them carefully. Find out the resident's condition at the time of discharge and if the resident will be using a wheelchair. Be positive and remind residents that their doctors believe they are ready to leave.

title: Isolation Precautions & PPE
summary: Isolation techniques, types of isolation, donning and doffing personal protective equipment (PPE).

sections.heading: Isolation Precautions Overview
sections.content: Isolation techniques include the use of protective barriers (an isolation room or unit), personal protective equipment (PPE) for caregivers and others entering the room, and limiting contact with the resident's personal items to keep germs from traveling into or out of the isolated area. Two reasons for ordering isolation: (1) To prevent the spread of disease, and (2) To protect residents with weakened immune systems from germs in the environment — called protective isolation or reverse isolation.

sections.heading: Infections and Isolation Types
sections.content: Contact Precautions (gown and gloves): MRSA/VRE, C-difficile, Shingles. Droplet Precautions (surgical mask): Influenza, Mumps, Rubella. Airborne Precautions (N95 respirator, negative pressure room): Tuberculosis. Standard Precautions apply to ALL residents at ALL times. Always use the correct precaution type for the specific infection.

sections.heading: Emotional Impact of Isolation
sections.content: Residents in isolation may need special attention because they are separated from everyone else. Feelings can include loneliness, confusion, and feeling 'dirty' because caregivers come into the room wearing protective attire. Be sure to give care with kindness and respect, check on them frequently, and answer call lights promptly. When possible, encourage the resident to talk about their concerns and offer diversions like television, radio, and reading material.

sections.heading: Isolation Guidelines for Linen and Trash
sections.content: Keep isolation trash containers covered. Keep isolation linen hamper covered. Wear gloves and other needed PPE when changing soiled linen. Do not set down dirty gloves in the room — dispose of them immediately. Never leave a soiled brief in the room. If a clean piece of linen drops on the floor, it becomes dirty and must be put in the isolation linen hamper labeled with the BIOHAZARD symbol. Keep clean and dirty linen from touching each other. DO NOT DROP DIRTY LINEN ON THE FLOOR. Handle contaminated linen as little as possible and avoid shaking. Post a sign at the door — use 'Please see nurse before entering room' rather than the word 'Isolation' to protect resident privacy.

sections.heading: Putting On and Removing Gloves
sections.content: Putting On Gloves: Wash and dry hands. Get a new pair for every resident. Slide one glove on, then use the gloved hand to slide the other glove in. Make sure gloves cover the wrists (or cover gown cuffs). Check for any damage. Removing Gloves: Grasp the outside of one glove below the cuff. Pull glove down so it turns inside out. Hold it with the still-gloved hand. Reach inside the other glove with two fingers. Pull it down over your hand and the first glove. Discard in appropriate container. Wash hands.

sections.heading: Putting On and Removing a Gown
sections.content: Putting On a Gown: Wash hands. Get a disposable gown. Let it unfold without touching any surface. Slide hands and arms through the sleeves. Tie the neck ties. Overlap the back to completely cover your uniform. Tie strings in a bow. Removing a Gown: Untie the waist ties. Pull apart neck ties or strips without touching hair. Loosen gown at the shoulders while holding the ties. Touch only the ties and the 'clean' inside surface. Pull the gown down from the shoulders and roll it inside out down your arms. Roll the gown into a ball (contaminated side inward) and dispose into the proper waste container. Wash hands.

sections.heading: Putting On and Removing a Mask
sections.content: Putting On a Mask: Wash hands. Pick up the mask by the elastic straps or top strings — do not touch the portion that covers your face. Place the mask over your nose and mouth (and under glasses). Tie the upper strings at the back with a bow, or place ear loops over ears. Be sure the lower edge covers your chin. Form the top edge of the mask to your nose. Removing a Mask: Wash hands. Untie the lower strings first, then untie the upper strings. Bring the upper strings together as you remove the mask. Replace the mask if it becomes soiled or moist. Wash hands after discarding.

sections.heading: PPE Order: Donning and Doffing
sections.content: Order for Putting ON PPE: Gown → Mask → Gloves. Order for Removing (DOFFING) PPE: Gloves → Gown → Mask. This order prevents contamination. Always wash hands after removing all PPE.

title: Bedmaking
summary: Rules for bedmaking, proper linen handling, making unoccupied and occupied beds.

sections.heading: Why Bedmaking Matters
sections.content: Beds are made every day. Clean, dry, and wrinkle-free beds promote comfort, prevent skin breakdown, and prevent pressure injuries. Beds are usually made in the morning or after baths — or while the resident is in the shower, up in the chair, or out of the room. In long-term care, linens are not changed every day. A complete linen change is usually done on the resident's bath or shower day — usually 1 or 2 times a week. Linens are always changed if wet, damp, soiled, or very wrinkled.

sections.heading: Rules for Bedmaking
sections.content: Use good body mechanics at all times. Follow the rules of medical asepsis. Follow Standard Precautions and the Bloodborne Pathogen Standard. Practice hand hygiene before handling clean linens. Bring only the linens you will need — you cannot use extra linens for another resident. Place clean linens on a clean surface — use the bedside chair, overbed table, or bedside stand. Place a barrier between the clean surface and the linens. Do not use extra linens in the resident's room for another resident — extra linens are considered contaminated.

sections.heading: Linen Handling
sections.content: When handling linens and making beds, practice medical asepsis. Your uniform is considered dirty — always hold linens away from your body and uniform. Never shake linens — shaking spreads microbes. Place clean linens on a clean surface — never put clean or used linens on the floor. Do not use torn or frayed linens. Never put used linens on the floor or on clean linens. Bag used linen in the room where they are used. Do not carry used linens un-bagged outside the resident's room. Follow Standard Precautions and the Bloodborne Pathogen Standard — contact with blood, body fluids, secretions, and excretions is likely.

sections.heading: Making an Unoccupied Bed
sections.content: Key steps: Gather all needed linens before starting. Raise the bed for proper body mechanics. Put on gloves. Remove used linens by rolling each piece away from you. Place each piece in a laundry bag. Move the mattress to the head of the bed. Place and tuck bottom sheet (center crease in middle, fan-fold to other side). Place the drawsheet and tuck. Go to the other side and pull all linens tight so there are no wrinkles. Place the top sheet, blanket, and bedspread. Tuck top linens together at the foot using mitered corners. Put on pillowcase. Place pillow with open end away from the door. Attach the call light. Lower the bed to a safe level. Complete a safety check. Wash hands.

sections.heading: Making an Occupied Bed
sections.content: The occupied bed is made while the resident is in the bed (due to a medical condition, the resident may not be permitted to get out of bed per doctor's order). Key steps: Knock, identify resident, introduce yourself, explain the procedure. Wash hands, provide for privacy. Raise bed to waist height and lock wheels. Loosen all top linen. Cover the resident with a bath blanket. Help the resident turn onto their side. Loosen bottom soiled linen on the working side. Roll soiled linen toward the resident. Place and tuck clean bottom linen. Have the resident roll over the bump to the completed side. Move to the other side. Remove soiled linen (never shake it, never put on floor). Pull clean linen through and tuck. Replace top sheet and blanket. Loosen linens over the resident's feet. Lower the head of the bed. Place the call light within reach. Wash hands.

title: Aging Process & Body Systems
summary: Physical changes during aging and common disorders of the integumentary, musculoskeletal, cardiovascular, respiratory, gastrointestinal, urinary, nervous, endocrine, and reproductive systems.

sections.heading: Integumentary System Changes
sections.content: As the body ages, the skin: becomes less elastic and loses strength, develops folds, lines, and wrinkles, shows brown spots (age spots or liver spots) on the wrists and hands, has fewer nerve endings and fewer blood vessels, thins and sags, becomes fragile and easily injured, becomes more dry and itchy, becomes more sensitive to cold and less sensitive to pain. Nails become thick and tough. Hair changes include whitening or graying, facial hair in some women, loss or thinning of hair, and drier hair.

sections.heading: Musculoskeletal System Changes
sections.content: Age-related changes: Muscles atrophy (shrink). Muscle strength, tone, and contractility decrease. Bone mass decreases, bones become weaker and more brittle. Vertebrae shorten. Joints become stiff and painful. Hip and knee joints become flexed. Gradual loss of height; trunk becomes shorter. Decreased mobility. Common disorders: Osteoarthritis — degeneration of the cartilage between bones; causes pain and aching at the joint, weakness, muscle wasting, and grating sounds with motion. Commonly occurs in fingers, knees, hips, and spine. Caused by age, joint injury, or obesity. Osteoporosis — loss of bone mass causing bones to become porous and brittle; signs include loss of height, hunchback, fatigue, pain, and bone fractures. Fractures — a broken bone; types include simple (bone broken in one place), open/compound (bone comes through skin), and closed (bone broken but skin intact).

sections.heading: Cardiovascular System Changes
sections.content: Age-related changes: Heart pumps with less force. Heart valves thicken and become stiff. Heart rate may slow. Abnormal heart rhythms may occur. Heart may enlarge slightly. Heart walls thicken. Arteries narrow and become stiffer. Less blood flows through narrowed arteries. Weakened heart works harder to pump blood. Number of red blood cells decreases. Common disorders: Myocardial Infarction (MI/heart attack) — caused by an inadequate blood supply to the heart, usually due to a blood clot; signs include severe chest pain radiating to the left jaw and arm, shortness of breath, sweating, blue lips, and vomiting. Hypertension (high blood pressure) — narrowing and hardening of blood vessels due to fatty plaque buildup; signs include blurred vision, headache, nosebleed, chest pain, flushed face, and weak pulse. Congestive Heart Failure (CHF) — heart cannot effectively pump blood, causing fluid backup; signs include shortness of breath, restlessness, edema, chest pain, and weight gain.

sections.heading: Respiratory System Changes
sections.content: Age-related changes: Respiratory muscles weaken. Some lung tissue is lost. Lung tissue becomes less elastic. Chest is less able to stretch and breathe. Difficulty breathing (dyspnea). Decreased strength for coughing and clearing the airway. Common disorders: Pneumonia — acute disease caused by bacteria, viruses, aspiration, or immobility; airways fill with fluid; signs include difficulty breathing, cough with sputum, rapid respiration, mental status changes, dehydration, and chest pain. Asthma — obstructive disorder causing bronchial inflammation; signs include rapid respirations, difficulty breathing, wheezing, rapid heart rate, and coughing. Emphysema — disease of the alveoli; air sacs permanently enlarged; signs include anxiety, weight loss, barrel chest, leaning forward to breathe, chronic cough, and wheezing.

sections.heading: Gastrointestinal & Urinary Changes
sections.content: Gastrointestinal changes: Decreased saliva production, difficulty swallowing (dysphagia), decreased appetite, decreased digestive juices, difficulty digesting fried and fatty foods, indigestion, loss of teeth, decreased peristalsis causing flatulence and constipation. Common disorders: Constipation — infrequent, hard, dry stools; caused by slow fecal movement. Fecal Impaction — prolonged buildup of feces in the rectum; liquid feces seep around the hardened mass. Diarrhea — frequent liquid stools; feces move rapidly reducing fluid absorption. GERD — chronic condition where stomach contents back up into the esophagus; signs include heartburn, sour taste, difficulty swallowing, and hoarseness. Urinary changes: Kidney function decreases, bladder muscles weaken, urinary frequency and urgency. UTI — caused by microorganisms entering the system; signs include frequency, urgency, dysuria, hematuria, and cloudy urine. Renal Failure — acute or chronic kidney failure; waste products not removed from blood; can be life-threatening.

sections.heading: Nervous System Changes
sections.content: Age-related changes: Brain and spinal cord lose nerve cells. Messages sent at a slower rate. Reflexes slow. Reduced blood flow to the brain. Shorter memory, forgetfulness, slower response, confusion, dizziness, sleep pattern changes, reduced sensitivity to pain and touch, decreased smell and taste, less tear secretion, decreased vision, hearing loss. Common nervous system disorders: Parkinson's Disease — progressive disorder affecting movement in persons over 50; signs include pill-rolling tremors, rigid stiff muscles, stooped posture, mask-like facial expression, and slow monotone speech. Multiple Sclerosis (MS) — central nervous system disease where the myelin covering nerve fibers is destroyed; affects women and Caucasians more; signs include blurred vision, muscle weakness, paralysis, tingling, balance problems, and depression. Seizures — violent, sudden contractions or tremors caused by abnormal electrical activity; types include partial (one part of brain), generalized tonic-clonic/grand mal, and generalized absence/petit mal. CVA/Stroke — caused by a burst or blocked blood vessel in the brain; if on the right side it affects the left side of the body, and vice versa; signs include sudden face drooping, arm weakness, speech difficulty — use FAST to identify.

sections.heading: Endocrine & Reproductive Changes
sections.content: Endocrine changes: Glands produce and secrete lower levels of hormones. Decreased function on the organs these hormones control. Common disorder — Diabetes Mellitus (DM): The pancreas fails to secrete enough insulin to handle sugar. Glucose remains in the bloodstream instead of getting into the cells. Signs include extreme thirst, excessive hunger, frequent urination, sugar in urine, irritability, blurred vision, slow wound healing, and fatigue. Hypoglycemia (low blood sugar) — caused by too little food, too much insulin, or heavy exercise; signs include hunger, perspiration, trembling, headache, paleness, rapid heartbeat, and sudden behavioral changes. Hyperglycemia (high blood sugar) — caused by undiagnosed diabetes, too much food, too little insulin, illness, or stress; signs include extreme thirst, rapid deep breathing, fruity breath, flushed cheeks, and confusion. Reproductive changes: Male — sperm production decreases, prostate gland enlarges (BPH). Female — menopause, decreased estrogen leads to possible osteoporosis, vaginal walls become drier.

sections.heading: Observation and Reporting
sections.content: The CNA is the first member of the interdisciplinary care team to make observations of the resident. Observations include but are not limited to: behaviors, skin condition, vital signs, level of pain, appetite, social interaction, and any change from normal. The CNA reports resident care and observations to the nurse. Always tell the resident's name and room number, the time observations were made or care was given, report only what you observed or did yourself, and give reports as often as the resident's condition requires or as often as requested by the nurse.

title: Pain Management
summary: Understanding pain, types of pain, signs and symptoms, reporting pain to the nurse, and measures to relieve pain.

sections.heading: Understanding Pain
sections.content: To have pain means to ache, hurt, or be sore. Pain is subjective — you cannot see, hear, touch, or smell a resident's pain. You must rely on what the resident tells you. Pain is personal — what hurts one resident may only ache to another. If a resident complains of pain, the resident has pain. Older persons may have decreased pain sensations or may ignore/deny new pain because they think it relates to an existing condition. Confused residents may not be able to verbally communicate pain — changes in usual behavior may indicate pain.

sections.heading: Types of Pain
sections.content: Acute Pain: Felt suddenly from injury, disease, trauma, or surgery. There is tissue damage. Lasts a short time (usually less than 6 months). Decreases with healing. Chronic Pain: Lasts longer than 6 months. Pain is constant or occurs off and on. There is no longer tissue damage. Remains long after healing. Arthritis and cancer are common causes. Radiating Pain: Felt at the site of tissue damage and in nearby areas. Pain from a heart attack is felt in the left side of the chest, jaw, and left arm. Phantom Pain: Felt in a body part that is no longer there — a resident with an amputated leg may still sense leg pain.

sections.heading: Signs and Symptoms of Pain
sections.content: Body responses include: increased pulse, respirations, and blood pressure, sweating (diaphoresis), nausea, vomiting, and pale skin (pallor). Behavioral signs include: changes in speech (slow or rapid, loud or quiet), crying, gasping, grimacing, groaning, grunting, holding the affected body part (splinting), irritability, maintaining one position and refusing to move, moaning, restlessness, rubbing, screaming, and quietness.

sections.heading: Reporting Pain to the Nurse
sections.content: When reporting pain, include: Location — where is the pain? Ask the resident to point to the area. Remember, pain can radiate — ask if pain is anywhere else. Onset and Duration — when did the pain start? How long has it lasted? Intensity — is it mild, moderate, or severe? Ask the resident to rate pain on a scale of 0-10, with 10 being the most severe. Description — ask the resident to describe the pain using their exact words (aching, burning, cramping, crushing, dull, gnawing, knifelike, piercing, pressure, sharp, sore, squeezing, stabbing, throbbing). Factors Causing Pain (precipitating factors) — what was the resident doing before the pain started? Other signs — dizziness, nausea, vomiting, weakness, numbness, or tingling.

sections.heading: Effects of Pain
sections.content: Unrelieved pain has serious effects on a resident's well-being: Increased anxiety, Lack of sleep, Decreased activity, Decreased appetite, Depression, Impaired mobility, and Isolation. Pain management is a resident right. The nurse can give pain-relief drugs to prevent or control pain. Preventing and controlling pain are easier than relieving pain — report signs and symptoms of pain at once.

sections.heading: Measures to Relieve Pain
sections.content: CNAs can help relieve pain by: Positioning the resident in good body alignment and using pillows for support. Using correct lifting, moving, and turning procedures. Waiting one-half hour after pain medication was given to perform care and procedures. Giving a back massage. Providing soft music to distract the resident. Avoiding sudden or jarring movements of the bed. Handling the resident gently. Applying heat or cold compresses as directed by the nurse. Providing a calm, quiet, darkened environment. Cultural diversity: Various cultures respond differently to pain. Some residents are stoic and show no reaction. Pain relief measures may have to be offered more than once.

title: End-of-Life Care
summary: Terminal illness, palliative and hospice care, the stages of dying, comfort needs, signs of death, and post-mortem care.

sections.heading: End-of-Life Care Overview
sections.content: End-of-life care describes the support and care given during the time surrounding death. Sometimes death is sudden; often it is expected. Some people gradually fail. End-of-life care may involve days, weeks, or months. One of the most difficult situations a CNA will face is the death of a resident. It is important that as a CNA you do not lose sight of the fact that you are still caring for the living. Hearing is the last sense to leave the body — even if a person is in a coma, always speak to them as if they may still be able to hear you. Until the resident takes their last breath, and even after, the resident needs to be treated with dignity and respect.

sections.heading: Terminal Illness & Types of Care
sections.content: A terminal illness is one from which the person will not likely recover — the disease or injury ends in death. Doctors cannot predict the exact time of death. Terminally ill residents can choose palliative care or hospice care. Palliative Care: Means to soothe or relieve. Involves relieving or reducing the intensity of uncomfortable symptoms without producing a cure. The focus is on relieving symptoms and treating the illness; the intent is to improve quality of life and provide family support. For anyone with a long-term illness that will cause death. Hospice Care: The focus is on the physical, emotional, social, and spiritual needs of dying persons and their families. Often the resident has less than 6 months to live. Cure or life-saving measures are not concerns. Pain relief and comfort are stressed. The goal is to improve quality of life.

sections.heading: The Stages of Dying (Kubler-Ross)
sections.content: Dr. Elizabeth Kubler-Ross described 5 stages of dying, also called the stages of grief. Grief is the person's response to loss. Stage 1: Denial — 'No, not me!' The person refuses to believe they are dying. Information about the illness is not heard. Stage 2: Anger — 'Why me?' There is anger and rage. Dying persons envy and resent those with life and health. Do not take the person's anger personally. Stage 3: Bargaining — 'Yes, me but...' Anger has passed. The person bargains with God or a higher power for more time. Stage 4: Depression — 'Yes, me.' The person is very sad and mourns lost things and the future loss of life. Stage 5: Acceptance — The person is calm, at peace, and accepts death. Unfinished business is complete. Note: Dying persons do not always pass through each stage, may never get beyond a certain stage, may move back and forth between stages, or may stay in one stage.

sections.heading: Comfort Needs of the Dying Resident
sections.content: Comfort is part of end-of-life care. It involves physical, mental, emotional, and spiritual needs. Goals are to prevent or relieve suffering and to respect end-of-life wishes. Physical needs: Dying takes a few minutes, hours, days, or weeks. Body processes slow. Every effort is made to promote physical and psychological comfort. Skin care, personal and oral hygiene, back massages, and good alignment promote comfort. Turn the person slowly and gently. Follow the care plan to prevent and control pain. Breathing problems (dyspnea) are common — Semi-Fowler's position and oxygen are helpful. An open window for fresh air may help. Noisy breathing called the 'death rattle' is common as death nears. Mental and emotional needs are very personal — some residents are calm; others are anxious or depressed. Simple measures such as touch, holding hands, back massage, soft lighting, and music at a low volume may be soothing. Spiritual needs: Provide privacy during prayer. Be courteous to the spiritual leader. Handle religious objects with care and respect.

sections.heading: Signs of Death & Signs That Death Is Near
sections.content: Signs that death is near include: restlessness and agitation, shortness of breath with pauses in breathing, depression, anxiety, drowsiness, confusion, constipation or incontinence, nausea and loss of appetite, healing problems, and swelling in hands, feet, or body areas. As death nears: Movement, muscle tone, and sensation are lost — starting in the feet and legs. Mouth muscles relax, the jaw drops, and the mouth may stay open. The face often looks peaceful. Body temperature rises, then the skin feels cool or cold, looks pale, and perspires heavily. The respiratory system fails — slow or rapid and shallow respirations are observed. Mucus collects in the airway — noisy, gurgling 'death rattle' breathing. Circulation fails — pulse is fast or slow, weak, and irregular; blood pressure starts to fall. The signs of death include: no pulse, no respirations, and no blood pressure. Pupils are fixed and dilated. The nurse calls the doctor to report the signs of death.

sections.heading: Post-Mortem Care & Caregiver Grief
sections.content: Post-mortem care is the care of the body after death. Care can begin after the resident has been pronounced dead by a physician or licensed medical professional (RN). Within 2 to 4 hours after death, rigor mortis occurs — muscles become rigid, joints stiffen, and blood ceases to circulate. For this reason it is important to align the body properly before rigor mortis occurs. Key post-mortem steps: Position the body supine with arms and legs straight. Close the eyes. Insert dentures if applicable. Close the mouth. Remove all jewelry except wedding rings. The nurse removes catheters and tubes. Bathe soiled areas. Place a clean gown on the body. Gather the person's belongings. Let the family view the body, then cover the body with a sheet. Report the time the body was taken by the funeral director, what was done with jewelry and personal items, and what was done with dentures. Tips for caregivers dealing with grief: Permit yourself to grieve over the loss of a resident you were fond of. Discuss your feelings about death with colleagues or a member of the clergy. Ask for help from staff and offer support to others who are grieving.

title: Personal Care Skills & Bathing
summary: Beginning and ending steps for every procedure, organizing and prioritizing care, and overview of bathing including complete bed bath.

sections.heading: Therapeutic Relationship in Personal Care
sections.content: A therapeutic relationship is a relationship between an individual who suffers from a disability or discomfort (resident) and a practitioner (caregiver) who possesses specific technical skill or knowledge and occupies a recognized role. Goals: To promote communication of distressing thoughts and feelings, to assist the resident with problem solving to help daily living, and to promote self-care and independence. Steps to establishing this relationship: Be concerned, be competent, be correct, be consistent, and be clear.

sections.heading: Beginning Steps for Every Procedure
sections.content: Follow these beginning steps before any personal care procedure: (1) Knock and announce yourself before entering the resident's room — residents have a right to privacy. (2) Introduce yourself by name and identify the resident by name. (3) Explain the procedure to the resident clearly, slowly, and directly. (4) Wash your hands — handwashing provides for infection prevention. It may need to be done more than once during a procedure. (5) Provide for the resident's privacy with a curtain, screen, or door — this is a legal right, not just a courtesy. (6) Adjust the bed to a safe level (usually waist high) and lock the bed wheels.

sections.heading: Ending Steps for Every Procedure
sections.content: Follow these ending steps after every personal care procedure: (1) Return bed to the lowest position and remove privacy measures. (2) Place the call light within the resident's reach — always respond to call lights promptly. (3) Wash your hands. (4) Report any changes in the resident to the nurse — you often spend the most time with a resident and are in the best position to note any changes. (5) Document the procedure using facility guidelines — after you have finished giving care, document using facility guidelines. Do not record care before it is given. If you do not document the care you gave, legally it did not happen. (6) In addition to these steps, remember to follow infection prevention guidelines and wear gloves or other PPE when appropriate.

sections.heading: Organizing, Prioritizing, and Flexibility
sections.content: Organizing: To arrange in working order. Prioritizing: To put things in order of importance — identify the most important things to get done and do these first. Flexibility: The ability to adapt to different circumstances. When caring for residents, you must manage your time well. The nurse creates work assignments based on the needs of the resident and availability of staff. Your responsibilities include: helping others when needed, never ignoring a resident who needs help, answering all call lights even when not assigned to a particular resident, and notifying the nurse if you cannot complete an assignment. Tools for managing time: Prioritize, make a schedule, combine activities (visit with residents while providing care), and get help when needed. Planning your work: Discuss priorities with the nurse, know the shift routine, list care or procedures on a schedule, estimate time needed for each task, plan care around mealtimes and activities, and identify when you will need co-worker assistance.

sections.heading: Overview of Bathing
sections.content: 3 main purposes for a bath: (1) To remove dirt and odor, (2) To stimulate circulation and provide exercise, (3) To relax the resident — if the experience causes anxiety or agitation, the nurse needs to be informed. As we age, skin becomes drier, thinner, and loses its elasticity. Bathing is important for healthy skin, observation of pressure areas, and good hygiene. Bathing Schedule: Most nursing homes schedule routine showers or tub baths. Schedules are on Resident Care Cards and Assignment Sheets. Privacy: Escort the resident fully clothed to the bathing area, screen with privacy curtains, and close doors to bathing areas. Water Temperature: Regulate water temperature to ensure the resident does not get scalded/burned and does not suffer from hypothermia. Skin observations during bathing — report to the nurse: dry or cracked skin, rashes, bruises, pale or reddened areas over bony parts, open skin areas, drainage or bleeding, and complaints of pain.

sections.heading: Complete Bed Bath
sections.content: A complete bed bath involves washing the resident's entire body in bed. Given to residents who are unconscious, paralyzed, in a cast or traction, or weak from illness or surgery. Key principles: Work from cleanest to dirtiest areas (cleanse face and eyes before the genital area). Uncover only one part of the body at a time. Wash, rinse, and dry one part of the body at a time. The resident participates as much as possible. Order: Eyes and face (no soap on face), ears and neck, arms and axillae, hands, chest and abdomen, legs and feet, back, then perineal care. Apply lotion to elbows and hands. After bath: Provide deodorant. Brush or comb hair. Help resident put on clean clothing. Return bed to lowest position. Place call light within reach. Complete safety check. Wash hands. Document the procedure. Report any changes to the nurse.

title: AM & PM Care
summary: Morning and evening care routines that prepare residents for the day and promote comfort before sleep.

sections.heading: AM Care Overview
sections.content: AM care is routine care given before breakfast to get residents ready for the day. It may be given by the night shift or day shift. AM care measures include: assisting residents to the bathroom or offering commodes, bedpans, or urinals. Cleaning incontinent residents and changing any wet or soiled linen. Helping residents wash their faces and hands. Assisting with oral hygiene. Positioning residents in bed or in a chair for breakfast. Brushing and combing hair. Straightening bed linens. Straightening the resident's room.

sections.heading: PM Care Overview
sections.content: PM care (also called HS care — hour of sleep) is care given at bedtime to promote comfort and relaxation before sleep. PM care measures include: assisting residents to the bathroom or offering commodes, bedpans, or urinals. Cleaning incontinent residents and changing any wet or soiled linen. Helping residents wash their faces and hands. Assisting with oral hygiene. Changing damp or soiled linens and straightening all other linens. Assisting the resident to undress and put on a gown or pajamas. Giving a back massage. Straightening the resident's room.

sections.heading: Incontinent Care During Routine
sections.content: Incontinent residents require extra attention during both AM and PM care. Wet or soiled linen must be changed immediately to prevent skin breakdown and infection. Clean the resident thoroughly and apply barrier cream as indicated in the care plan. Always maintain the resident's dignity and privacy during incontinent care. Report any skin redness, irritation, or breakdown to the nurse.

title: Shower, Tub & Partial Bath
summary: Safety measures and procedures for giving showers, tub/whirlpool baths, and partial bed baths.

sections.heading: Shower Safety Measures
sections.content: Key safety rules for showers: Make sure the shower is clean before and after use. Make sure the floor of the shower room is dry. Place a bath mat on the floor unless there are non-skid strips or a nonskid floor. Cover the resident for warmth and privacy, including during transport to and from the shower room. Place needed items within reach of the resident, including the call light. Adjust water temperature and pressure before the resident gets into the shower to avoid chilling or burning the resident. If the resident uses a shower chair, position the chair before adjusting the water. Use the hand-held nozzle to direct water away from the resident. Avoid using bath oils — they make shower surfaces slippery. Do not leave weak, unsteady, or confused residents alone. Stay within hearing distance if the resident can be left alone.

sections.heading: Tub and Whirlpool Safety Measures
sections.content: Key safety rules for tub and whirlpool baths: Make sure the tub is clean before and after use. Make sure the floor of the bathroom is dry. Place a bath mat on the floor unless there are non-skid strips or a nonskid floor. Cover the resident for warmth and privacy, including during transport. Place needed items within reach of the resident, including the call light. Check that grab bars, hydraulic lifts, and other safety aids are in working order. Drain the tub before the resident gets out — keep the resident covered to protect from exposure and chilling. Have the resident use safety bars when getting into and out of the tub. Avoid using bath oils — they make tub surfaces slippery. Do not leave weak, unsteady, or confused residents alone. Stay within hearing distance if the resident can be left alone.

sections.heading: Partial Bed Bath
sections.content: A partial bed bath involves bathing only certain areas of the body. Areas bathed include: Face. Hands. Arms and armpits (axillae). Chest. Abdomen. Back. Perineal area. Buttocks. A partial bed bath may be given to refresh the resident, remove odor, and prevent skin breakdown. It is used when a full bath is not possible or necessary. Follow beginning and ending steps for all procedures. Maintain privacy and dignity throughout.
sections.videoTitle: Provide Partial Bed Bath and Backrub

title: Hair Care & Grooming
summary: Grooming overview, hair and scalp conditions, brushing, combing, and shampooing techniques.

sections.heading: Overview of Grooming
sections.content: Hair care, shaving, nail and foot care, and clean garments prevent infection and promote comfort. Such measures affect love, belonging, and self-esteem needs. People differ in their grooming measures — some only want clean hair while others want a certain hairstyle; some want only clean hands while others want polished nails. Men may shave and groom their beards. Women may shave their legs and underarms. Some women use hair removal methods for facial hair. Allow the resident to perform grooming measures to the extent possible — this promotes independence and quality of life. The nursing process reflects the person's culture, personal choice, skin and scalp conditions, health history, and self-care ability.

sections.heading: Hair and Scalp Conditions
sections.content: Common hair and scalp conditions: Alopecia — hair loss that may be complete or partial; causes include heredity (male pattern baldness), cancer treatments, skin disease, stress, poor nutrition, pregnancy, some drugs, and hormone changes. Dandruff — excessive dry white flakes from the scalp; itching is common; sometimes eyebrows and ear canals are involved; medicated shampoos correct the problem. Pediculosis (Lice) — infestation with wingless insects; lice attach their eggs (nits) to hair shafts; nits are oval and yellow to white in color, hatching in about 1 week; adult lice are tan to grayish-white and about the size of a sesame seed; lice spread through clothing, head coverings, furniture, towels, bed linens, and sexual contact; treated with medicated shampoo, lotions, and creams; thorough bathing and washing clothing and linens in hot water are required. Report open areas, crusts, dandruff, or unusual hair loss to the nurse.

sections.heading: Brushing and Combing Hair
sections.content: Brushing and combing hair are part of morning care; some people also do so at bedtime. Provide hair care when needed and before visitors arrive. Encourage residents to do their own hair care — the resident chooses how to brush, comb, and style; assist as needed. Brushing increases blood flow to the scalp and brings scalp oils along the hair shaft to keep hair soft and shiny. Daily brushing and combing prevent tangled and matted hair. Start at the hair ends, then work toward the scalp. For curly, coarse, and dry hair use a wide-tooth comb; start at the neckline, working upward, lift and fluff hair outward. Wet hair or apply conditioner, petroleum jelly, or other hair care product as per the care plan. Braiding prevents long hair from matting and tangling — you need the resident's consent. NEVER cut the resident's hair. Report matted or tangled hair to the nurse.

sections.heading: Shampooing
sections.content: Shampoo frequency varies — 1, 2, or 3 times a week or daily depending on hair and scalp condition, hairstyle, and personal choice. The nurse tells you what shampoo method to use based on the resident's condition, safety factors, and personal choice. Shampoo methods include: During shower or tub bath — use a hand-held nozzle directed at the hair. At the sink — resident sits or lies facing away from the sink; a folded towel protects the neck; use a water pitcher or hand-held nozzle to wet and rinse. In bed — resident's head and shoulders are at the edge of the bed; a shampoo tray is under the head to protect linens; tray drains into a basin on a chair; use a water pitcher; used for residents needing complete bed rest. Commercial shampoo caps — contain a cleaning agent that does not need rinsing; warm the cap per manufacturer's instructions; check temperature; apply to head; massage gently for 1 to 3 minutes; remove cap; dry and comb hair. If a hairdresser does the shampooing, do not shampoo the hair — the resident wears a shower cap during the shower.

title: Oral Hygiene & Denture Care
summary: Mouth care, periodontal disease prevention, care for unconscious residents, and denture cleaning and storage.

sections.heading: Oral Hygiene Benefits and Risks
sections.content: Oral hygiene (mouth care) keeps the mouth and teeth clean, prevents mouth odors and infections, increases comfort, makes food taste better, and reduces the risk for cavities and periodontal disease. Periodontal disease (gum disease) is an inflammation of tissues around the teeth caused by plaque and tartar buildup. Plaque is a thin film containing saliva, microbes, and other substances that causes tooth decay (cavities). Hardened plaque is called tartar — it builds up at the gum line, causes red and swollen gums that bleed easily, destroys bone, and leads to tooth loss. Illness, disease, and some drugs cause a bad taste in the mouth, whitish coating on the tongue, redness and swelling in the mouth, and dry mouth. Dry mouth is also common from oxygen, smoking, decreased fluid intake, and anxiety. Follow Standard Precautions — you may have contact with mucous membranes and gums may bleed during mouth care.

sections.heading: When and How Often to Provide Mouth Care
sections.content: Assist with oral hygiene after sleep, after meals, and at bedtime. Many people practice oral hygiene before meals. Some residents need mouth care every 2 hours or more often. Conditions requiring more frequent mouth care include: unconscious residents, dying residents, and residents with tube feedings. Report these conditions to the nurse: irritation, raised areas, coated or swollen tongue, ulcers (canker sores — small, painful, white sores), flaky white spots, dry or cracked or bleeding or chapped lips, loose or chipped or broken or decayed teeth, swollen or irritated or bleeding or whitish gums, breath that smells bad or fruity, and resident reports of mouth pain.

sections.heading: Oral Care for the Unconscious Resident
sections.content: Unconscious residents cannot eat or drink. Some breathe with their mouths open and many receive oxygen — these factors cause mouth dryness and crusting on the tongue and mucous membranes. Oral hygiene keeps the mouth clean and moist and helps prevent infection. Use sponge swabs to apply the cleaning agent. Apply a lubricant to the lips after cleaning to prevent cracking. To prevent aspiration (breathing fluid, food, vomitus, or an object into the lungs): position the resident on one side with the head turned well to the side so excess fluid runs out of the mouth; use only a small amount of fluid to clean the mouth; do not insert dentures — they are not worn when the resident is unconscious. Always assume that unconscious residents can hear — explain what you are doing step-by-step, and tell the resident when you are done, leaving, and when you will return.

sections.heading: Denture Care
sections.content: A denture is an artificial tooth or set of artificial teeth (false teeth) that replace missing teeth due to gum disease, tooth decay, or injury. Full dentures replace all upper or lower teeth; partial dentures replace missing teeth when some natural teeth remain. Mouth care is given and dentures cleaned as often as natural teeth. Dentures are slippery when wet and easily break or chip if dropped. Hold them firmly when removing or inserting them. During cleaning, hold them firmly over a basin of water lined with a towel. Hot water causes dentures to warp — store in cool or warm water or denture soaking solution when not worn. Dentures are usually removed at bedtime; rinse before reinserting. Remind residents not to wrap dentures in tissues or napkins — they are easily discarded. Privacy is important — allow privacy when the resident cleans dentures and return them as quickly as possible.

title: Shaving, Nail & Foot Care
summary: Safe shaving techniques, hand and nail care, and foot care including when to involve a podiatrist.

sections.heading: Shaving a Resident
sections.content: Many men want a clean-shaven face for comfort and mental well-being. Female residents may wish to have their legs and underarms shaved periodically. Some women need facial hair removed — this is a dignity issue; do not shave a woman's facial hair unless she requests it. Some residents use electric shavers, which are cleaned between uses. Other residents prefer blade shavers — razor blades can cause nicks or cuts; follow Universal Precautions. Razor blades and disposable razors are discarded into a sharps container. Blade shavers are NOT used on residents receiving anticoagulant therapy. Razors should never be shared between residents. Report these observations to the nurse: nicks or cuts that do not stop bleeding, open areas or sores, rashes, skin irritation or redness.

sections.heading: Hand and Nail Care
sections.content: Hangnails, ingrown nails, and nails torn away from the skin cause skin breaks that are portals of entry for microbes. Long or broken nails can scratch skin or snag clothing. Cleaning and trimming fingernails are easier after soaking. Use nail clippers to cut fingernails — never use scissors. Be careful not to damage surrounding tissue when clipping. The same nail equipment should not be used on more than one resident. Report these observations: reddened, irritated, or calloused areas; breaks in the skin; changes in color of the skin or nails; hot or cold hands.

sections.heading: Foot Care
sections.content: Healthy feet are important for mobility and comfort. Hangnails, ingrown nails, and nails torn away from the skin cause skin breaks that are portals of entry for microbes. Dirty feet, socks, or stockings harbor microbes and cause odors. Shoes and socks provide a warm, moist environment for the growth of microbes. Injuries to the feet occur from stubbing toes, stepping on sharp objects, being stepped on, and poorly fitting shoes that cause blisters. Healing is prolonged in residents with poor circulation due to diabetes or vascular disease. Infections or foot injuries are particularly serious for elderly residents with circulatory disorders — gangrene and amputation are serious complications. Trimming and clipping toenails can easily result in injury — nursing assistants should NOT cut or trim toenails; toenails are trimmed by a Podiatrist (foot doctor). Report: reddened, irritated, or calloused areas; breaks in the skin; changes in color of the skin or nails; hot or cold feet.

title: Dressing & Undressing
summary: Proper techniques for assisting residents with dressing and undressing, including adaptive equipment and prosthesis care.

sections.heading: Overview of Dressing
sections.content: Some residents may need help dressing, including those with physical impairments. Residents wear street clothing during the day and change into a gown or pajamas for bed. Incontinent residents may change more often. Your goal is to make the dressing experience a positive one. Allow and encourage the resident to be involved as much as possible. Respect the resident's right to choose their own clothing. For residents with impaired decision-making, offer a choice between two appropriate outfits.
sections.videoTitle: Dress a Resident with a Weak Arm

sections.heading: Clothing and Affected Side Rule
sections.content: Daywear for women: bra, undergarment, slip or camisole, dress or pant outfit, stockings or socks, and non-skid shoes. Men: boxer shorts or briefs, undershirt, pants, shirt, socks, and non-skid shoes. Nightwear: gown or pajamas with non-skid socks. Residents may have a weaker side of the body due to stroke or injury — called the affected side. Never refer to it as the bad side. When dressing, place the weaker arm through a sleeve first, then help with the stronger arm. When undressing, start with the stronger (unaffected) side first. For weak legs, have the resident sit down to pull pants over both legs.

sections.heading: Adaptive Equipment
sections.content: Dressing can be adapted to promote independence by utilizing adaptive clothing or devices when part of the resident's care plan. Devices may include: Shoe horn — assists with putting on shoes. Dressing Stick — helps pull clothing over limbs. Reacher — extends reach for items. Sock Aid — assists in donning socks without bending. Button Hook — aids in fastening buttons. Residents who need minimal assistance benefit from having clothing and devices laid out on the bed or easily available in closets.

sections.heading: Glasses and Hearing Aids
sections.content: Glasses — when taking on and off, hold carefully at the temples with both hands. Never grab by the lenses. Keep in protective case when not worn. Rinse with warm water before cleaning, then use a soft cloth. Hearing Aids — amplify sound to help residents hear better, but also amplify background noise. Care: clean the ear mold with soap and water daily and dry thoroughly. Turn to the Off position when not in use. Remove the battery at night. Check that batteries are inserted correctly.

sections.heading: Artificial Limb (Prosthesis) Care
sections.content: A prosthesis should fit snugly with no room for movement up and down. When removing the prosthesis, look for reddened areas on the stump. Notify the nurse of any sores, drainage, or very red areas. The resident should have no sensation of the stump hitting the bottom of the prosthesis while standing or walking. Do not allow the resident to walk with the prosthesis if this occurs until the problem is resolved. If the prosthesis liner or sock becomes wet or soiled, do not allow the resident to use it until dry.

title: The Urinary System
summary: Anatomy and function of the urinary system, age-related changes, common conditions including UTI, incontinence, and renal failure.

sections.heading: Urinary System Anatomy
sections.content: The Urinary System includes: 2 Kidneys — filter blood and fluids, remove waste products, form urine, regulate water excretion, maintain fluid balance. 2 Ureters — carry urine from the kidneys to the bladder. Bladder — collects and holds urine until it is released. Urethra — urine is eliminated from the body through the urethra.

sections.heading: Age-Related Changes
sections.content: Kidney function decreases with age. Reduced blood supply to kidneys. Kidneys atrophy. Bladder tissue becomes less able to stretch. Bladder muscles weaken. Bladder may not empty completely. Urinary frequency and urgency may occur. Urinary incontinence may occur. Night-time urination (nocturia) may increase.

sections.heading: Urinary Tract Infections (UTI)
sections.content: UTIs are caused by a microorganism entering and infecting the system. More common in women due to a shorter urethra. Causes: catheter placement, contamination of vaginal area with fecal matter, backflow of urine from indwelling catheter, poor perineal hygiene, immobility, poor fluid intake. Signs and symptoms: urinary frequency, oliguria (scant urine), urgency, dysuria (painful urination), hematuria (blood in urine), cloudy urine, urine odor, lower abdominal discomfort, pelvic pain in women, rectal pain in men, changes in mood or behavior.

sections.heading: Renal Failure & Common Elimination Problems
sections.content: Renal Failure — kidneys do not function or are severely impaired. Waste products accumulate in the blood. Fluid is retained. Heart failure and hypertension can result. Acute renal failure is sudden (severe injury, bleeding, heart attack). Chronic Kidney Disease (CKD) — kidney function is gradually lost over years; caused by hypertension and diabetes. Signs of CKD: nausea, vomiting, fatigue, sleep problems, decreased urine output, muscle cramps. Common elimination problems: dysuria (painful urination), hematuria (blood in urine), nocturia (frequent night urination), urgency, frequency, incontinence (inability to control urine loss).

title: The Reproductive System
summary: Female and male reproductive anatomy, age-related changes, and common disorders including vaginitis, BPH, and prostate cancer.

sections.heading: Female Reproductive Anatomy
sections.content: Ovaries (2) — almond-shaped glands that produce the ovum (egg), secrete estrogen and progesterone, and release the ovum during ovulation. Fallopian Tubes (2) — guide the ovum into the uterus where it may be fertilized. Uterus — hollow, pear-shaped muscular organ in the pelvic cavity; provides for growth and nourishment of the fetus during pregnancy. Vagina — muscular tube extending from the vaginal opening to the cervix; receives the penis during fertilization and serves as the birth canal. External genitalia (vulva) — includes the labia and clitoris. Breasts (2) — mammary glands that produce milk after birth.

sections.heading: Male Reproductive Anatomy
sections.content: Penis — cylinder-shaped organ made of erectile tissue that becomes enlarged with sexual arousal. Testicles (2, testes) — produce sperm cells and seminal fluid. Scrotum — sac that contains the testes. Seminal Vesicles (2) — store sperm and secrete fluid added to semen. Prostate Gland — adds a secretion that increases the movement of sperm cells.

sections.heading: Age-Related Changes
sections.content: Male changes: sperm production decreases, prostate gland enlarges which can interfere with urination. Female changes: menstruation ends (menopause is when a female stops having menstrual periods), decreased estrogen may lead to loss of calcium causing brittle bones and osteoporosis, vaginal walls become drier and thinner.

sections.heading: Common Disorders
sections.content: Vaginitis — infection of the vagina caused by bacteria, protozoa, or fungus (yeast). Signs: change in color, odor, and amount of vaginal discharge accompanied by itching and burning. Treatment: oral medications, vaginal creams, or vaginal suppositories. Benign Prostatic Hypertrophy (BPH) — prostate becomes enlarged in aging men, pressing on the urethra. Leads to: frequent urination, dribbling, difficulty starting urine flow, urinary retention, and risk of UTI. Treated with medication or surgery. Prostate Cancer — most common cancer in males and second leading cause of death among men; treatable in early stages with surgery, radiation, chemotherapy, and hormone therapy.

title: Perineal Care & Elimination
summary: Perineal care procedures, bedpan and urinal assistance, commode use, incontinence management, and enemas.

sections.heading: Perineal Care
sections.content: Perineal care involves cleaning the genital and anal areas. Performed to: prevent infections, prevent odors, and promote comfort. Perform at least daily during the bath, whenever the area is soiled with urine or feces, and before or after certain procedures. Provide perineal care only when the resident is unable to do it themselves. Ensure privacy at all times. Use different unsoiled areas of the washcloth for each stroke. Change water and rinse washcloths as needed. For uncircumcised males, replace the foreskin after retracting for cleaning. Provide care with total respect and dignity.

sections.heading: Bedpan and Urinal
sections.content: A bedpan is usually made of plastic and can be used by either male or female residents who cannot get out of bed. A urinal is made of plastic for male residents to urinate. A fracture pan with a thinner rim is available when the regular bedpan is too large. Elimination equipment is kept in the bathroom between uses and must be labeled for shared bathrooms. Equipment must never be placed on overbed tables or side tables. Urine and feces are infectious waste — always wear gloves and avoid spills or splashes.
sections.videoTitle: Assist a Resident with a Bedpan

sections.heading: Bedside Commode
sections.content: A bedside commode has a toilet seat with a container and allows a normal position for elimination. Residents unable to walk to the bathroom often use commodes. Some commodes are wheeled into the bathroom and placed over the toilet — remove the container when used with the toilet. Make sure commode wheels are locked and it is positioned properly over the toilet.

sections.heading: Incontinence and Enemas
sections.content: Urinary incontinence is the involuntary loss of urine. Types: urge, stress, overflow. Causes: medications, constipation, UTI, enlarged prostate, neurological disorders, aging changes. Problems: embarrassment, skin irritation, UTIs, pressure ulcers. Observe and report: red or irritated skin, open areas, rash, peeling skin, odor. Enemas are ordered by a doctor and introduced into the rectum to remove feces or relieve constipation. Types: cleansing enemas (tap water, soapsuds, saline) and commercial enemas (oil-retention, Fleet). Bowel incontinence causes: constipation, diarrhea, muscle or nerve damage, surgery, rectal cancer.

title: Urinary Catheter Care
summary: Types of urinary catheters, closed drainage system components, infection control principles, drainage bag positioning, leg bags, and intake and output measurement.

sections.heading: Types of Urinary Catheters
sections.content: A urinary catheter is a rubber or plastic tube placed through the urethral opening into the bladder to drain urine. Catheter insertion is performed by a nurse or doctor. Types: Straight catheter — inserted to drain the bladder and then removed. Indwelling catheter (retention or Foley catheter) — inserted and left in the bladder so urine drains constantly into a drainage bag. External catheter (condom catheter) — a soft rubber sheath that slides over the penis; tubing connects it to a drainage bag.
sections.videoTitle: Care for a Patient with a Urinary Catheter

sections.heading: Closed Urinary System
sections.content: Parts of a closed urinary system: Catheter — rubber or plastic tube placed through the urethral opening into the bladder. Balloon — located near the tip of the catheter, inflated to keep the catheter in the bladder. Tubing — connects the catheter to the collection bag. Drainage bag — collects urine drained from the bladder. Portals of entry for germs: raising the catheter bag above the bladder, allowing the drainage bag to lie on the floor, disconnecting the tubing, using the same measuring container for multiple residents, not cleaning tubing when changing from drainage bag to leg bag.

sections.heading: Drainage Bag Positioning
sections.content: The drainage bag hangs from the bed frame, chair, or wheelchair. It must not touch the floor. The bag must always be lower than the level of the resident's bladder to prevent backflow. Leg bags are smaller bags strapped to the thigh and used when the resident is mobile or out of bed.

sections.heading: Intake and Output (I&O)
sections.content: Intake — the amount of fluid taken in. An adult needs 1500 ml of water daily to survive; approximately 2000–2500 ml per day is required to maintain a normal fluid balance. Output — the amount of fluid lost. An adult excretes approximately 1500 ml of fluid per day. Measure all intake: oral fluids, IV fluids, and tube feedings. Measure all output: urine, emesis (vomit), wound drainage, and liquid stool. Use a graduate (measuring cup) to measure urine. Record all measurements in milliliters (ml) on the I&O sheet. Report to the nurse if urine output falls below 30 ml per hour, or if there are sudden changes in amounts.
sections.videoTitle: How to Apply a Condom Catheter

title: Digestive System & GI Disorders
summary: Digestive system anatomy, aging changes, common disorders including constipation, fecal impaction, GERD, and colostomy care.

sections.heading: Digestive System Anatomy
sections.content: Mouth — receives food and prepares it for digestion. Tongue — aids in chewing and swallowing. Salivary glands — secrete saliva to moisten food and begin digestion. Pharynx (throat) — muscular tube that pushes food into the esophagus. Esophagus — muscular tube carrying food down into the stomach. Stomach — muscular, pouch-like sac that stirs and churns food into smaller particles. Small intestine — about 20 feet long; includes duodenum (digestive juices added, including bile from liver and pancreatic juices), jejunum, and ileum (where food is absorbed). Large intestine — absorbs water from digested food, leaving semi-solid feces. Anus — feces pass out of the body through the anus.

sections.heading: Age-Related Changes & Common Disorders
sections.content: Age-related GI changes: decreased saliva production, difficulty swallowing (dysphagia), decreased appetite, decreased digestive juices, difficulty digesting fried and fatty foods, indigestion, loss of teeth, decreased peristalsis causing flatulence and constipation. Constipation — passage of a hard, dry stool; resident strains to have a BM; caused by slow movement of feces through the bowel. Signs: infrequent BMs (less than every 2 days), pain during BM, hard ball-like stool, loss of appetite. Factors affecting bowel elimination: privacy, diet (fiber-rich foods help), fluids (6–8 glasses of water daily), activity (exercise stimulates peristalsis), age, and medications.

sections.heading: Fecal Impaction and Diarrhea
sections.content: Fecal impaction — prolonged retention and buildup of feces in the rectum. Results when constipation is not relieved. The person cannot have a BM. Liquid feces pass around the hardened mass and seep from the anus. Signs: abdominal discomfort, abdominal distention (swelling), nausea, cramping, rectal pain. Diarrhea — frequent passage of liquid stools. Feces move rapidly through the intestines, reducing fluid absorption. Urgent need for BM. Signs: abdominal cramping, nausea, vomiting.

sections.heading: GERD and Colostomy Care
sections.content: GERD (Gastroesophageal Reflux Disease) — chronic condition where stomach contents back up into the esophagus. Can inflame and damage the esophageal lining, cause bleeding, ulcers, and narrowing. Signs: heartburn or regurgitation, sour taste in mouth, dysphagia, lump in throat, hoarseness, sore throat, dry cough. Colostomy — surgical creation of an artificial opening (stoma) between the colon and abdominal wall. Ileostomy — between ileum and abdominal wall. Stool consistency depends on which section of colon is brought to the surface. Provide skin care around the stoma and report any signs of irritation. Be sensitive to psychosocial issues: embarrassment, body image, depression, social withdrawal.

title: Specimen Collection
summary: Collection methods and procedures for urine, stool, and sputum specimens.

sections.heading: Urine Specimens
sections.content: Urine specimens are collected for urine tests to help doctors make a diagnosis or evaluate treatment. They are studied for blood, microbes, and abnormal cells. Random urine specimen — collected for urinalysis at any time; no special measures needed. Clean catch urine specimen — the perineal area is cleaned before collection to reduce microbes in the urine during specimen collection.
sections.videoTitle: Midstream Clean Catch Urine Sample Collection

sections.heading: Stool and Sputum Specimens
sections.content: Stool specimens are collected for various tests; studied for blood, microbes, fat, worms, or parasites. Doctors use results to make a diagnosis or evaluate treatment. Sputum specimens — respiratory disorders cause the lungs, bronchi, and trachea to produce mucus. Mucus expelled through the mouth is called sputum. Sputum specimens are studied for blood, microbes, and abnormal cells.

title: Types of Wounds
summary: Classification of wounds, wound characteristics, and key observations CNAs must recognize and report.

sections.heading: Wound Classification
sections.content: Wounds are classified by cause, depth, and whether the skin is intact. Open wound — break in the skin or mucous membrane; examples include cuts, lacerations, abrasions, punctures, and surgical incisions. Closed wound — skin is intact but underlying tissue is damaged; examples include contusions (bruises) and hematomas. Acute wound — heals in a predictable time frame; usually caused by surgery or trauma. Chronic wound — does not heal in the expected time; often associated with poor circulation, infection, or underlying disease.

sections.heading: Wound Depth & Tissue Involvement
sections.content: Superficial wound — involves only the epidermis (outermost skin layer); heals quickly with minimal intervention. Partial-thickness wound — involves the epidermis and part of the dermis; may have blistering or moist wound bed. Full-thickness wound — extends through the dermis into subcutaneous tissue, muscle, or bone; requires more complex care. Undermining — tissue destruction beneath intact skin at the wound edges. Tunneling — narrow channel of tissue destruction extending from the wound.

sections.heading: Common Wound Types
sections.content: Abrasion — scraping of the skin surface. Laceration — jagged, irregular tear in the skin. Incision — clean cut made by a sharp instrument (such as a surgical scalpel). Puncture — deep wound caused by a sharp, pointed object. Avulsion — tearing away of skin or tissue. Pressure injury — localized damage to skin and underlying tissue over a bony prominence from pressure or shear. Venous ulcer — wound caused by poor venous circulation, usually on the lower leg. Diabetic foot ulcer — wound caused by neuropathy and poor circulation in people with diabetes.

title: Pressure Injuries
summary: Staging, risk factors, prevention measures, and nursing assistant responsibilities for pressure injuries.

sections.heading: What is a Pressure Injury?
sections.content: A pressure injury (formerly called a pressure ulcer or bedsore) is localized damage to the skin and underlying soft tissue. It usually occurs over a bony prominence or is related to a medical device or other object. Common bony prominences at risk: sacrum. Coccyx. Heels. Hips (greater trochanter). Ankles (lateral malleolus). Shoulder blades. Elbows. Back of the head. Ears.

sections.heading: Pressure Injury Staging
sections.content: Stage 1 — intact skin with non-blanchable redness; the area may be painful, firm, soft, warmer, or cooler compared to adjacent tissue. Stage 2 — partial-thickness loss of skin with exposed dermis; wound bed is pink/red and moist; may appear as an intact or ruptured blister. Stage 3 — full-thickness skin loss; subcutaneous fat may be visible; no bone, tendon, or muscle is exposed; slough may be present. Stage 4 — full-thickness skin and tissue loss with exposed bone, tendon, or muscle; slough or eschar may be present. Unstageable — full-thickness skin and tissue loss where the base is covered by slough or eschar; true depth cannot be determined until removed. Deep Tissue Pressure Injury (DTPI) — persistent non-blanchable deep red, maroon, or purple discoloration; may be difficult to detect in darkly pigmented skin.

sections.heading: Risk Factors & Prevention
sections.content: Risk factors: immobility and inability to reposition. Incontinence (moisture damages skin). Poor nutrition and hydration. Decreased sensation. Altered mental status. Friction and shear forces during transfers. Prevention measures: reposition the resident at least every 2 hours (or per care plan). Keep skin clean and dry. Apply barrier cream to incontinent residents per care plan. Use pressure-relieving mattresses and seat cushions. Perform and document skin inspections with each repositioning. Report any redness, skin breakdown, or changes to the nurse immediately. Ensure adequate nutrition and fluid intake. Use proper transfer and lifting techniques to reduce shear.

title: Wound Care & Dressings
summary: Wound observations, dressing change principles, infection signs, and what to report to the nurse.

sections.heading: Wound Observations to Report
sections.content: Always report the following to the nurse: location and size of the wound. Color of the wound bed (red, pink, yellow, black). Presence of slough (yellow, tan, gray soft dead tissue) or eschar (black, brown, tan hard dead tissue). Type and amount of drainage (exudate). Odor from the wound. Condition of the surrounding skin (periwound) — redness, warmth, swelling, maceration. Pain or increased tenderness at the wound site. Any new skin breakdown or changes in an existing wound. Status of the dressing — saturated, displaced, or missing.

sections.heading: Wound Drainage (Exudate) Types
sections.content: Serous — clear, watery fluid; normal in the early stages of healing. Serosanguineous — pale pink, watery mixture of serum and blood; also normal during healing. Sanguineous — bright red bloody drainage; indicates active bleeding. Purulent — thick, opaque, yellow, green, or brown; indicates infection — report immediately. Amount descriptors: scant (minimal), small, moderate, large (copious).

sections.heading: Dressing Change Principles & Infection Signs
sections.content: CNA role in dressing changes: follow the nurse's instructions and care plan exactly. Perform hand hygiene before and after. Use standard precautions (gloves, mask if splashing is possible). Remove old dressing gently to avoid damaging fragile tissue. Dispose of soiled dressings in a biohazard bag. Never clean or pack a wound unless specifically trained and delegated. Signs of wound infection: increased redness, warmth, or swelling around wound. Purulent (pus-like) drainage. Foul odor. Increased pain or tenderness. Wound edges not coming together or wound enlarging. Fever or chills in the resident. Report any signs of infection to the nurse immediately.

title: Endocrine System & Diabetes
summary: Structure and function of the endocrine system, diabetes types, signs of hypo/hyperglycemia, complications, and CNA care guidelines.

sections.heading: The Endocrine System
sections.content: The endocrine system is made up of glands in different areas of the body. Glands are organs that produce and secrete chemicals called hormones. Hormones are chemical substances created by the body to control numerous body functions. Functions include: maintains homeostasis (internal stability). Influences growth and development. Regulates levels of sugar in the blood. Regulates level of calcium in the bones. Regulates the body's ability to reproduce. Determines how fast cells burn food for energy. Common aging change: endocrine glands produce and secrete lower levels of hormones, resulting in decreased function in the organs these hormones control.

sections.heading: Hypothyroidism
sections.content: Hypothyroidism, or under-activity of the thyroid gland, may cause a variety of symptoms and may affect all body functions. The body's normal rate of functioning slows, causing mental and physical sluggishness. Symptoms may vary from mild to severe.

sections.heading: Diabetes Mellitus — Types
sections.content: Type 1 Diabetes: usually diagnosed in children and young adults. The pancreas does not produce any insulin. The condition continues throughout the person's life. Managed with daily injections of insulin or an insulin pump and a special diet. Regular blood glucose testing must be done. Type 2 Diabetes: the most common form of diabetes. Either the body does not produce enough insulin or the body fails to properly use the insulin (insulin resistance). Usually develops slowly after age 35. Often occurs in obese people or those with a family history. Can usually be controlled with diet and/or oral medications. Gestational Diabetes: develops during pregnancy, usually disappears after birth. However, the woman remains at risk for type 2 diabetes.

sections.heading: Signs & Symptoms of Diabetes
sections.content: General signs and symptoms: extreme thirst. Excessive hunger. Frequent urination. Sugar in the urine. Blurred vision. Fatigue and drowsiness. Changes in behavior such as irritability. Hypoglycemia (low blood sugar) — occurs when there is too much insulin in the bloodstream: hunger. Perspiration. Trembling and anxiety. Headache. Paleness, cold and clammy feeling. Rapid heartbeat. Mental confusion. Sudden change in behavior or irritability. Blurred or double vision. Feeling of faintness, weakness, or fatigue. Loss of consciousness. Hyperglycemia (high blood sugar) — occurs with too much sugar in the blood: extreme thirst and frequent urination. Labored, rapid and deep breathing. Breath that smells sweet or fruity. Blurred vision. Flushed cheeks. Dry skin. Mental confusion. Nausea, vomiting and abdominal pain. Drowsiness or loss of consciousness.

sections.heading: Complications from Diabetes
sections.content: Changes in the circulatory system can cause heart attack and stroke, reduced circulation, poor wound healing, and kidney and nerve damage. Damage to the eyes can cause vision loss and blindness. Poor circulation and impaired wound healing may cause leg and foot ulcers, infected wounds, and gangrene. Gangrene can lead to amputation.

sections.heading: CNA Guidelines for Diabetes Care
sections.content: Follow diet instructions exactly. The intake of carbohydrates — including breads, potatoes, grains, pastas, and sugars — must be regulated. Meals must be eaten at the same time each day. The resident must eat all of what is served. If a resident refuses to eat or is not following the diet, notify the nurse. Encourage the resident to follow their exercise program. Regular exercise affects how quickly bodies use food and improves circulation. Observe the resident's management of insulin. Doses are calculated exactly and given at the same time each day. There must be a balance between insulin level and food intake. Proper foot care is vitally important. People with diabetes have poor circulation; even a small sore can become a large wound leading to amputation. Encourage comfortable, well-fitting leather shoes. Residents should never go barefoot. Cotton socks are best. Only a doctor or podiatrist should trim a diabetic's toenails.

title: Nutrition & Balanced Diet
summary: Essential nutrients, major food groups, fluid balance, dehydration vs. edema, and age-related changes that affect nutrition.

sections.heading: Nutrition Basics
sections.content: Nutrients are any of the substances we need to take into our bodies for life and growth. Nutrition is the study of these nutrients and how the body uses food. Food functions: aids in the repair and growth of tissues. Provides the body with energy. Makes the various body systems function properly. A well-balanced diet includes adequate water and liquids as well as foods from all the major food groups: breads and grains. Bright colored vegetables. Deep colored fruits. Low-fat and non-fat dairy products. Dried beans, nuts, fish, poultry, lean meats, and eggs.

sections.heading: Major Nutrients
sections.content: Fats: give the body energy. Examples include dairy, meats, and nuts. Proteins: build and repair tissues. Examples include dairy, meats, and vegetables. Carbohydrates: provide energy and fiber. Examples include grains, cereal, vegetables, and fruits. Vitamins and Minerals: enable proper functioning of eyes, nerves, skin, heart, bones, liver, kidneys, and teeth. Found in dark green and yellow vegetables, fruits, dry beans, and fish.

sections.heading: Food Pyramid Levels
sections.content: Water/Liquids (base): essential every day. Older adults should drink at least eight 8-ounce glasses of liquids per day unless medically restricted. Breads, Cereal, and Rice: provides proteins, Vitamin B, iron, magnesium, carbohydrates, and fiber. Recommended 6 or more servings per day. Whole grains are important for energy. Fruits and Vegetables: provide Vitamins A and C, carbohydrates, fiber, and other nutrients. Recommended 3 or more servings of vegetables and 2 or more of fruits daily. Dairy and Dry Beans/Meats: provide proteins, fat, iron, and carbohydrates. Dairy also provides calcium and riboflavin. Recommended 2 or more servings from each group daily. Fats, Oils, Salts, and Sweets: eat sparingly. Use liquid vegetable oils and soft spreads low in saturated and trans fat.

sections.heading: Age-Related Changes Affecting Nutrition
sections.content: As people age, they need fewer calories to maintain their weight. However, the need for nutrient-rich food remains the same or increases. Older adults may have fewer teeth or problems with dentures. They may exercise less, and their bodies may digest and absorb certain nutrients less efficiently. Older adults may also develop chewing problems, chronic illness, or lack of appetite. Encourage foods that provide the most nutrients per serving rather than foods high in starch, fats, and empty calories. Loss of appetite can occur due to illness, pain, medications, depression, anxiety, or unpleasant smells.

sections.heading: Fluid Balance
sections.content: Water is essential to life, second only to oxygen. The standard amount of fluid recommended per person is 8 to 10 glasses (2,000 to 2,600 ml) every day. The amount of fluid taken in and the amount lost must be equal. If fluid intake exceeds fluid output, body tissues swell with water — this is called edema. Edema is common in people with heart and kidney disease. If fluid output exceeds fluid intake, there is a decrease in water in tissues — this is called dehydration. Dehydration is a serious medical condition that can cause confusion and is life-threatening. Signs of dehydration: dry skin, sunken eyes, constipation, confusion, fatigue, dark urine. Special fluid guidelines: Force fluids — encourage extra fluids. Restrict fluids — limit the amount of fluids. NPO — nothing by mouth. Fluid can be lost through urine, feces, skin, lungs, vomit, and wound drainage.

title: Therapeutic & Mechanical Diets
summary: Types of therapeutic diets ordered by physicians, mechanical and texture-modified diets, thickened liquids, and nutritional supplements.

sections.heading: Therapeutic Diets
sections.content: CNAs need to be aware of any special or therapeutic diets ordered by the physician. Therapeutic diets may be ordered because of a nutritional deficiency or a disease, to eliminate or decrease certain substances in the diet, or for weight control. A resident's meal should always be checked prior to serving to ensure the proper diet is being served. Calorie Restricted Diet: calorie intake is reduced below the minimum daily requirements. Reason: weight reduction. Foods: low in fat and carbohydrates, lean meats; avoid butter, cream, gravies, cakes, pastries, candy. Sodium Restricted Diet: sodium or salt are removed or limited to prevent or decrease fluid retention. Fruits and vegetables and unsalted foods are allowed; salted and high-sodium foods are not. Low Fat Diet: decreases the amount of fat in the diet. Reason: heart disease, high cholesterol. Foods: skim milk, gelatin, sherbet, lean meat, poultry, fish, rice, pasta, bread, cereal, vegetables. Low Cholesterol Diet: limits or removes foods containing a large amount of cholesterol. Reason: heart disease, high cholesterol.
